style_notes.hard_questions: relevant distractors with minimal recycling, including partially correct College Board-style traps
style_notes.answer_keys: specific explanations for incorrect choices that identify the exact misconception

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'organelle'?
choices: a molecule that speeds up a chemical reaction
the entire fluid portion of the cell outside the membrane
a membrane-bound or non-membrane-bound structure inside a cell that performs a specific function
a tissue made of many similar cells
questions.explain: Organelle is a membrane-bound or non-membrane-bound structure inside a cell that performs a specific function.
choice_explanations: This choice describes an enzyme, not an organelle.
This choice describes cytoplasm, not an organelle.
Correct. Organelle is a membrane-bound or non-membrane-bound structure inside a cell that performs a specific function.
This choice describes a tissue level of organization, not a cell structure.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'plasma membrane'?
choices: the rigid outer layer found in all cells
the selectively permeable boundary made mainly of phospholipids and proteins that surrounds the cell
the region of DNA that controls transcription
the internal scaffold made of protein filaments
questions.explain: Plasma membrane is the selectively permeable boundary made mainly of phospholipids and proteins that surrounds the cell.
choice_explanations: This is tempting because some cells also have cell walls, but the plasma membrane is not a rigid outer layer and is present in all cells.
Correct. Plasma membrane is the selectively permeable boundary made mainly of phospholipids and proteins that surrounds the cell.
This describes a promoter, which regulates gene expression rather than enclosing the cell.
This describes the cytoskeleton, which supports cell shape but does not form the cell's boundary.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'phospholipid bilayer'?
choices: a double layer of proteins that pumps water through the cell
two layers of phospholipids arranged with hydrophilic heads outward and hydrophobic tails inward
two layers of carbohydrates that store energy in the membrane
a single layer of phospholipids with tails facing the cytosol
questions.explain: Phospholipid bilayer is two layers of phospholipids arranged with hydrophilic heads outward and hydrophobic tails inward.
choice_explanations: Membranes contain proteins, but the basic bilayer is made of phospholipids, not a double layer of proteins.
Correct. Phospholipid bilayer is two layers of phospholipids arranged with hydrophilic heads outward and hydrophobic tails inward.
Carbohydrates can attach to membrane molecules, but they do not form the main membrane structure.
A membrane is not a single layer, and the tails face inward toward each other rather than toward the cytosol only.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'selective permeability'?
choices: the process by which vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane
the movement of water across a membrane toward lower solute concentration
the ability of a membrane to block all molecules equally
the property of a membrane that allows some substances to cross more easily than others
questions.explain: Selective permeability is the property of a membrane that allows some substances to cross more easily than others.
choice_explanations: This defines exocytosis, a bulk transport mechanism rather than the property of the membrane.
This defines osmosis, which is one consequence of membrane permeability, not the term itself.
A selectively permeable membrane does not block everything equally; it discriminates among substances.
Correct. Selective permeability is the property of a membrane that allows some substances to cross more easily than others.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'fluid mosaic model'?
choices: the idea that membranes are rigid walls made mostly of cellulose
the model describing DNA as a double helix of nucleotides
the theory that all membrane proteins are permanently fixed in one place
the model describing membranes as a dynamic phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins moving within it
questions.explain: Fluid mosaic model is the model describing membranes as a dynamic phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins moving within it.
choice_explanations: Cellulose is part of plant cell walls, not plasma membranes.
This choice describes DNA structure, not membrane structure.
Some proteins are anchored, but the fluid mosaic model emphasizes that many membrane components can move laterally.
Correct. Fluid mosaic model is the model describing membranes as a dynamic phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins moving within it.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'integral membrane protein'?
choices: a membrane carbohydrate attached only to the outer surface
a soluble enzyme found only in the nucleus
a membrane protein embedded within the lipid bilayer, often spanning part or all of it
a protein filament that makes up the cytoskeleton
questions.explain: Integral membrane protein is a membrane protein embedded within the lipid bilayer, often spanning part or all of it.
choice_explanations: This describes a carbohydrate side chain or glycoprotein component, not an integral protein.
Integral membrane proteins are not defined by being soluble or nuclear.
Correct. Integral membrane protein is a membrane protein embedded within the lipid bilayer, often spanning part or all of it.
This describes cytoskeletal proteins, which may interact with membranes but are not embedded in the bilayer by definition.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'peripheral membrane protein'?
choices: a carbohydrate chain attached to a membrane lipid
a protein loosely associated with the membrane surface rather than embedded in the bilayer
a phospholipid with one hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic tails
a protein that spans the membrane and forms a channel
questions.explain: Peripheral membrane protein is a protein loosely associated with the membrane surface rather than embedded in the bilayer.
choice_explanations: This describes a glycolipid component, not a peripheral membrane protein.
Correct. Peripheral membrane protein is a protein loosely associated with the membrane surface rather than embedded in the bilayer.
This describes a phospholipid, not a protein.
That describes an integral membrane protein, not a peripheral one.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'glycoprotein'?
choices: a membrane protein with attached carbohydrate chains used in cell recognition or signaling
a carbohydrate stored in the Golgi apparatus for energy
a membrane lipid with attached phosphate groups
a protein filament that helps move vesicles
questions.explain: Glycoprotein is a membrane protein with attached carbohydrate chains used in cell recognition or signaling.
choice_explanations: Correct. Glycoprotein is a membrane protein with attached carbohydrate chains used in cell recognition or signaling.
The Golgi modifies glycoproteins, but a glycoprotein is not simply stored carbohydrate.
This describes a phospholipid, not a glycoprotein.
This describes cytoskeletal motor systems, not cell-recognition molecules.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'glycolipid'?
choices: a carbohydrate monomer that diffuses through the bilayer
a membrane lipid with an attached carbohydrate chain that can contribute to cell recognition
a lipid used exclusively for long-term energy storage
a membrane protein that transports glucose into the cell
questions.explain: Glycolipid is a membrane lipid with an attached carbohydrate chain that can contribute to cell recognition.
choice_explanations: A carbohydrate monomer is not the same as a glycolipid and generally does not diffuse freely through the membrane core.
Correct. Glycolipid is a membrane lipid with an attached carbohydrate chain that can contribute to cell recognition.
Some lipids store energy, but a glycolipid is a membrane component with a carbohydrate attached.
A glucose transporter is a protein, not a glycolipid.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'cholesterol'?
choices: the protein that uses ATP to move ions against their gradients
the pigment that captures light in chloroplast membranes
the carbohydrate that forms channels for water movement
a membrane lipid that helps stabilize membrane fluidity in animal cells
questions.explain: Cholesterol is a membrane lipid that helps stabilize membrane fluidity in animal cells.
choice_explanations: This describes an active transport protein, not cholesterol.
This describes chlorophyll, not cholesterol.
Water channels are proteins such as aquaporins, not carbohydrates.
Correct. Cholesterol is a membrane lipid that helps stabilize membrane fluidity in animal cells.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'diffusion'?
choices: the ingestion of large particles by the cell
the net movement of particles from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration
the ATP-driven movement of solutes across a membrane
the movement of water from lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration
questions.explain: Diffusion is the net movement of particles from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration.
choice_explanations: That describes phagocytosis, a type of endocytosis, not diffusion.
Correct. Diffusion is the net movement of particles from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration.
That describes active transport, not diffusion.
That is osmosis specifically, not the broader term diffusion.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'osmosis'?
choices: the movement of solute particles from low concentration to high concentration
the loss of water by evaporation from a leaf surface
the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane
the ATP-powered pumping of water through membrane proteins
questions.explain: Osmosis is the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane.
choice_explanations: This describes movement against a gradient and focuses on solutes, not water diffusion.
This describes transpiration, not osmosis.
Correct. Osmosis is the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane.
Water movement itself is passive; ATP is not directly required for osmosis.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'facilitated diffusion'?
choices: the packaging of materials into vesicles for export
the passive movement of water through the lipid bilayer only
the movement of substances against their gradient using ATP
the passive movement of substances down their concentration gradient through membrane proteins
questions.explain: Facilitated diffusion is the passive movement of substances down their concentration gradient through membrane proteins.
choice_explanations: This describes exocytosis, not transport through membrane proteins.
Some water moves through aquaporins, but facilitated diffusion is not limited to water or to the bilayer alone.
This describes active transport, which moves substances against a gradient.
Correct. Facilitated diffusion is the passive movement of substances down their concentration gradient through membrane proteins.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'active transport'?
choices: the direct passage of all ions through phospholipids
the movement of substances across a membrane against their gradient using energy
the passive movement of molecules into equilibrium
the movement of water down its own concentration gradient
questions.explain: Active transport is the movement of substances across a membrane against their gradient using energy.
choice_explanations: Ions do not pass directly through the hydrophobic core easily; active transport uses proteins.
Correct. Active transport is the movement of substances across a membrane against their gradient using energy.
Passive movement toward equilibrium is the opposite of active transport.
This describes osmosis, not active transport.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'concentration gradient'?
choices: a difference in the concentration of a substance across space or across a membrane
the point at which enzymes reach their maximum rate
the energy stored in ATP bonds
a region where all molecules are at equal concentration
questions.explain: Concentration gradient is a difference in the concentration of a substance across space or across a membrane.
choice_explanations: Correct. Concentration gradient is a difference in the concentration of a substance across space or across a membrane.
This choice confuses concentration gradient with enzyme saturation.
ATP energy is not the definition of a concentration gradient.
Equal concentration means no gradient exists.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'electrochemical gradient'?
choices: the movement of phospholipids within the membrane
the attraction between water molecules and ions in solution
the energy released when ATP is hydrolyzed
the combined effect of a solute's concentration difference and electrical charge difference across a membrane
questions.explain: Electrochemical gradient is the combined effect of a solute's concentration difference and electrical charge difference across a membrane.
choice_explanations: This describes membrane fluidity, not ion gradients.
This describes hydration interactions, not an electrochemical gradient.
ATP hydrolysis can power transport, but it is not the gradient itself.
Correct. Electrochemical gradient is the combined effect of a solute's concentration difference and electrical charge difference across a membrane.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'transport protein'?
choices: a membrane protein that helps specific substances cross the membrane
a chromosome-associated protein that condenses DNA
a cytosolic protein that digests macromolecules
a ribosomal protein that builds peptide bonds
questions.explain: Transport protein is a membrane protein that helps specific substances cross the membrane.
choice_explanations: Correct. Transport protein is a membrane protein that helps specific substances cross the membrane.
This describes histone-related DNA packaging roles, not transport across membranes.
Digestive enzymes can affect cellular chemistry, but they do not define transport proteins.
Ribosomal proteins function in translation, not membrane transport.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'channel protein'?
choices: a protein that seals vesicles after endocytosis
a receptor that always uses ATP to move ligands
a phospholipid that traps ions in the membrane
a membrane protein that forms a hydrophilic passageway through which specific substances can move
questions.explain: Channel protein is a membrane protein that forms a hydrophilic passageway through which specific substances can move.
choice_explanations: This does not define a channel protein.
Receptors bind signals; they do not necessarily transport ligands, and ATP use is not part of channel definition.
Phospholipids do not form selective hydrophilic passageways for ions.
Correct. Channel protein is a membrane protein that forms a hydrophilic passageway through which specific substances can move.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'carrier protein'?
choices: a protein that permanently holds open a pore in the membrane
a membrane carbohydrate that labels the cell for immune recognition
a protein that makes ATP in the mitochondrion
a membrane protein that changes shape to move a specific substance across the membrane
questions.explain: Carrier protein is a membrane protein that changes shape to move a specific substance across the membrane.
choice_explanations: A permanently open pore is more like a channel; carrier proteins bind and change conformation.
That describes a recognition role, not a carrier protein.
ATP synthase is not the general definition of a carrier protein.
Correct. Carrier protein is a membrane protein that changes shape to move a specific substance across the membrane.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'aquaporin'?
choices: a pump that uses ATP to move sodium ions
a carbohydrate that stores water inside vacuoles
a phospholipid that repels water from the membrane surface
a membrane channel protein that specifically facilitates the movement of water
questions.explain: Aquaporin is a membrane channel protein that specifically facilitates the movement of water.
choice_explanations: This describes an ion pump, not a water channel.
Carbohydrates do not store water in this way and do not define aquaporins.
Phospholipids contribute to membrane structure but are not water channels.
Correct. Aquaporin is a membrane channel protein that specifically facilitates the movement of water.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'hypertonic'?
choices: describing a solution with a higher solute concentration than another solution
describing a membrane that allows only water to pass
describing a solution with a lower solute concentration than another solution
describing a solution with the same solute concentration as another solution
questions.explain: Hypertonic is describing a solution with a higher solute concentration than another solution.
choice_explanations: Correct. Hypertonic is describing a solution with a higher solute concentration than another solution.
This describes selective permeability, not tonicity.
That defines hypotonic, the opposite of hypertonic.
That defines isotonic, not hypertonic.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'hypotonic'?
choices: describing a condition in which no net water movement occurs
describing a solution with a lower solute concentration than another solution
describing a membrane protein that moves glucose
describing a solution with more dissolved solute than the cell
questions.explain: Hypotonic is describing a solution with a lower solute concentration than another solution.
choice_explanations: No net water movement occurs in an isotonic comparison, not because a solution is hypotonic by definition.
Correct. Hypotonic is describing a solution with a lower solute concentration than another solution.
This choice is unrelated to tonicity and describes a transport protein.
That describes a hypertonic solution, not a hypotonic one.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'isotonic'?
choices: describing a membrane that is impermeable to all solutes
describing two solutions with equal solute concentrations, resulting in no net water movement
describing a solution that causes a cell to lyse
describing a solution with fewer solutes than the cell
questions.explain: Isotonic is describing two solutions with equal solute concentrations, resulting in no net water movement.
choice_explanations: Membrane permeability is a different concept from tonicity.
Correct. Isotonic is describing two solutions with equal solute concentrations, resulting in no net water movement.
Cells may lyse in a hypotonic environment, not simply because two solutions are isotonic.
A solution with fewer solutes than the cell is hypotonic, not isotonic.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'tonicity'?
choices: the ability of a surrounding solution to cause a cell to gain or lose water
the total number of organelles in a cell
the rate at which ATP is produced by mitochondria
the net movement of proteins through the Golgi apparatus
questions.explain: Tonicity is the ability of a surrounding solution to cause a cell to gain or lose water.
choice_explanations: Correct. Tonicity is the ability of a surrounding solution to cause a cell to gain or lose water.
This is unrelated to tonicity and concerns cell structure count.
ATP production rate is a metabolic concept, not tonicity.
This concerns protein trafficking, not water balance.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'solute'?
choices: the membrane separating two solutions
a substance dissolved in a solvent
the liquid that does the dissolving
the enzyme that speeds diffusion
questions.explain: Solute is a substance dissolved in a solvent.
choice_explanations: A membrane can separate solutions, but it is not the dissolved substance.
Correct. Solute is a substance dissolved in a solvent.
This defines solvent, not solute.
There is no enzyme whose general role is to speed diffusion in this way; this is unrelated.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'solvent'?
choices: the dissolving agent of a solution
the substance being dissolved
the total concentration of all dissolved particles
the membrane protein that transports ions
questions.explain: Solvent is the dissolving agent of a solution.
choice_explanations: Correct. Solvent is the dissolving agent of a solution.
This defines solute, not solvent.
This refers to concentration or osmolarity, not solvent.
This describes a transport protein, not the dissolving medium.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'vesicle'?
choices: a protein channel that opens when a ligand binds
a folded membrane in the mitochondrion
a DNA region where transcription begins
a small membrane-bound sac used to transport or store materials in the cell
questions.explain: Vesicle is a small membrane-bound sac used to transport or store materials in the cell.
choice_explanations: This describes a gated channel protein, not a vesicle.
This describes a crista, not a vesicle.
This describes a promoter, not a vesicle.
Correct. Vesicle is a small membrane-bound sac used to transport or store materials in the cell.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'endocytosis'?
choices: the process by which a cell takes in materials by forming vesicles from the plasma membrane
the breakdown of glucose to pyruvate
the passive movement of molecules down a gradient
the release of materials by vesicle fusion with the membrane
questions.explain: Endocytosis is the process by which a cell takes in materials by forming vesicles from the plasma membrane.
choice_explanations: Correct. Endocytosis is the process by which a cell takes in materials by forming vesicles from the plasma membrane.
This describes glycolysis, not membrane transport.
This describes diffusion, not bulk membrane uptake.
This describes exocytosis, the opposite direction of bulk transport.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'exocytosis'?
choices: the passive movement of ions through channels
the uptake of materials by membrane infolding
the process by which a cell releases materials when vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane
the engulfing of a bacterium by a white blood cell specifically
questions.explain: Exocytosis is the process by which a cell releases materials when vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane.
choice_explanations: This is membrane transport, but not vesicle-mediated secretion.
This describes endocytosis, not exocytosis.
Correct. Exocytosis is the process by which a cell releases materials when vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane.
That is a specific type of endocytosis called phagocytosis, not the definition of exocytosis.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'phagocytosis'?
choices: a type of endocytosis in which the cell engulfs large particles or whole cells
the release of neurotransmitters from a vesicle
the passive movement of oxygen through the membrane
the uptake of extracellular fluid in tiny vesicles
questions.explain: Phagocytosis is a type of endocytosis in which the cell engulfs large particles or whole cells.
choice_explanations: Correct. Phagocytosis is a type of endocytosis in which the cell engulfs large particles or whole cells.
This is exocytosis, not phagocytosis.
This describes simple diffusion, not endocytosis.
This describes pinocytosis, which involves fluid rather than large particles.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'pinocytosis'?
choices: the movement of water through aquaporins
the engulfing of large particles such as bacteria
a type of endocytosis in which the cell takes in extracellular fluid and dissolved substances
the export of proteins from the Golgi
questions.explain: Pinocytosis is a type of endocytosis in which the cell takes in extracellular fluid and dissolved substances.
choice_explanations: This is facilitated diffusion of water, not pinocytosis.
This describes phagocytosis, not pinocytosis.
Correct. Pinocytosis is a type of endocytosis in which the cell takes in extracellular fluid and dissolved substances.
This is exocytosis or vesicle trafficking, not fluid uptake.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'receptor-mediated endocytosis'?
choices: a transport process that allows any nearby solute to diffuse inward equally
the direct ATP-powered pumping of receptor proteins into the cell
the breakdown of receptors by ribosomes in the cytosol
a selective form of endocytosis triggered when specific ligands bind to membrane receptors
questions.explain: Receptor-mediated endocytosis is a selective form of endocytosis triggered when specific ligands bind to membrane receptors.
choice_explanations: This is the opposite of selective uptake; receptor-mediated endocytosis depends on specificity.
ATP may be involved in vesicle processes, but receptors are not simply pumped inward by a membrane pump.
Ribosomes synthesize proteins; they do not break down membrane receptors.
Correct. Receptor-mediated endocytosis is a selective form of endocytosis triggered when specific ligands bind to membrane receptors.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'surface area-to-volume ratio'?
choices: the ratio of DNA to cytoplasm inside the nucleus
the amount of ATP used per square micrometer of membrane
the proportion of proteins to phospholipids in a membrane
the amount of surface area available for exchange relative to the volume of a cell
questions.explain: Surface area-to-volume ratio is the amount of surface area available for exchange relative to the volume of a cell.
choice_explanations: This is unrelated to exchange across the cell surface.
ATP use can affect transport but is not what SA:V ratio measures.
Membrane composition is a different concept from geometric exchange ratio.
Correct. Surface area-to-volume ratio is the amount of surface area available for exchange relative to the volume of a cell.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'cell wall'?
choices: the internal membrane network that synthesizes lipids
the flexible phospholipid boundary present in all cells
a rigid outer layer outside the plasma membrane that provides support and protection in many organisms
the protein scaffold that anchors organelles
questions.explain: Cell wall is a rigid outer layer outside the plasma membrane that provides support and protection in many organisms.
choice_explanations: This describes smooth ER, not a cell wall.
This describes the plasma membrane, not the cell wall.
Correct. Cell wall is a rigid outer layer outside the plasma membrane that provides support and protection in many organisms.
This describes the cytoskeleton, not a cell wall.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'cytoplasm'?
choices: the membrane that encloses lysosomes
the fluid found only inside the nucleus
the structure that organizes spindle fibers during division
the contents of the cell between the plasma membrane and the nucleus, including the cytosol and organelles
questions.explain: Cytoplasm is the contents of the cell between the plasma membrane and the nucleus, including the cytosol and organelles.
choice_explanations: This is a membrane component of one organelle, not the whole cytoplasmic region.
This describes nucleoplasm, not cytoplasm.
This describes the centrosome/centrioles, not cytoplasm.
Correct. Cytoplasm is the contents of the cell between the plasma membrane and the nucleus, including the cytosol and organelles.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'cytosol'?
choices: the semifluid portion of the cytoplasm in which organelles are suspended
the set of protein fibers that shapes the cell
the rigid external layer of a plant cell
the entire contents of the cell including the nucleus
questions.explain: Cytosol is the semifluid portion of the cytoplasm in which organelles are suspended.
choice_explanations: Correct. Cytosol is the semifluid portion of the cytoplasm in which organelles are suspended.
This describes the cytoskeleton, not the aqueous fluid portion.
This describes a cell wall, not cytosol.
Cytosol is only the fluid component, not the entire cell contents.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'nucleus'?
choices: the organelle that modifies and ships proteins
the structure that stores digestive enzymes for intracellular breakdown
the organelle that makes ATP from food molecules
the membrane-bound organelle that contains most of the cell's DNA in eukaryotes
questions.explain: Nucleus is the membrane-bound organelle that contains most of the cell's DNA in eukaryotes.
choice_explanations: This describes the Golgi apparatus, not the nucleus.
This describes lysosomes, not the nucleus.
This describes mitochondria, not the nucleus.
Correct. Nucleus is the membrane-bound organelle that contains most of the cell's DNA in eukaryotes.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'nucleolus'?
choices: the region of the cell where ATP is synthesized
the membrane opening that regulates traffic into the nucleus
the dense region within the nucleus where rRNA is made and ribosome subunits are assembled
the site where lipids are synthesized
questions.explain: Nucleolus is the dense region within the nucleus where rRNA is made and ribosome subunits are assembled.
choice_explanations: ATP synthesis occurs mainly in mitochondria or chloroplasts, not the nucleolus.
This describes the nuclear pore or envelope function, not the nucleolus.
Correct. Nucleolus is the dense region within the nucleus where rRNA is made and ribosome subunits are assembled.
This describes smooth ER, not the nucleolus.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'ribosome'?
choices: the cellular structure that assembles amino acids into proteins
the organelle that digests old cell parts with hydrolytic enzymes
the DNA region where replication begins
the membrane channel that transports water
questions.explain: Ribosome is the cellular structure that assembles amino acids into proteins.
choice_explanations: Correct. Ribosome is the cellular structure that assembles amino acids into proteins.
This describes a lysosome, not a ribosome.
This describes an origin of replication, not the protein-synthesizing machinery.
This describes an aquaporin, not a ribosome.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'rough endoplasmic reticulum'?
choices: the membrane network studded with ribosomes that synthesizes and begins processing secreted or membrane proteins
the organelle that packages proteins after they leave the cell
the site of ATP generation in aerobic respiration
the membrane network that mainly synthesizes lipids and detoxifies drugs
questions.explain: Rough endoplasmic reticulum is the membrane network studded with ribosomes that synthesizes and begins processing secreted or membrane proteins.
choice_explanations: Correct. Rough endoplasmic reticulum is the membrane network studded with ribosomes that synthesizes and begins processing secreted or membrane proteins.
Packaging and sorting occur in the Golgi, not after proteins leave the cell.
This describes mitochondria, not rough ER.
This describes smooth ER, not rough ER.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'smooth endoplasmic reticulum'?
choices: the cytoskeletal fibers that shorten during muscle contraction
the membrane stacks that modify proteins for secretion
the membrane network lacking ribosomes that synthesizes lipids and helps detoxify certain substances
the organelle that contains chlorophyll
questions.explain: Smooth endoplasmic reticulum is the membrane network lacking ribosomes that synthesizes lipids and helps detoxify certain substances.
choice_explanations: This describes actin microfilaments, not smooth ER.
This describes the Golgi apparatus, not smooth ER.
Correct. Smooth endoplasmic reticulum is the membrane network lacking ribosomes that synthesizes lipids and helps detoxify certain substances.
This describes chloroplasts, not smooth ER.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'Golgi apparatus'?
choices: the structure that forms spindle fibers from tubulin
the membrane network where translation of secreted proteins begins
the organelle that modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids into vesicles
the organelle that contains digestive enzymes in an acidic environment
questions.explain: Golgi apparatus is the organelle that modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids into vesicles.
choice_explanations: This describes centrosome-related microtubule organization, not the Golgi apparatus.
This describes rough ER, not the Golgi.
Correct. Golgi apparatus is the organelle that modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids into vesicles.
This describes lysosomes, not the Golgi.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'lysosome'?
choices: a storage organelle that maintains plant turgor pressure
an acidic membrane-bound organelle containing enzymes that digest macromolecules and worn-out cell parts
a vesicle that carries mRNA out of the nucleus
the site where cellulose is synthesized for the cell wall
questions.explain: Lysosome is an acidic membrane-bound organelle containing enzymes that digest macromolecules and worn-out cell parts.
choice_explanations: This describes the central vacuole, not a lysosome.
Correct. Lysosome is an acidic membrane-bound organelle containing enzymes that digest macromolecules and worn-out cell parts.
mRNA exits through nuclear pores, not lysosomal vesicles.
Cellulose synthesis is not the function of lysosomes.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'vacuole'?
choices: the cytoskeletal structure that causes chromosome movement
the organelle that converts light energy into chemical energy
a membrane-bound sac used for storage, water balance, or digestion, especially large in many plant cells
the protein gate that allows facilitated diffusion
questions.explain: Vacuole is a membrane-bound sac used for storage, water balance, or digestion, especially large in many plant cells.
choice_explanations: This refers to spindle/microtubule function, not a vacuole.
This describes a chloroplast, not a vacuole.
Correct. Vacuole is a membrane-bound sac used for storage, water balance, or digestion, especially large in many plant cells.
This describes a channel or carrier protein, not a vacuole.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'central vacuole'?
choices: the opening in the cell wall through which adjacent cells exchange materials
the network that adds carbohydrates to proteins for secretion
the organelle that performs cellular respiration
the large plant cell organelle that stores water and helps maintain turgor pressure
questions.explain: Central vacuole is the large plant cell organelle that stores water and helps maintain turgor pressure.
choice_explanations: This describes plasmodesmata, not the central vacuole.
This describes rough ER/Golgi processing, not the central vacuole.
This describes mitochondria, not the central vacuole.
Correct. Central vacuole is the large plant cell organelle that stores water and helps maintain turgor pressure.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'mitochondrion'?
choices: the membrane stack that modifies proteins for export
the organelle that stores genetic information in prokaryotes
the organelle in which aerobic cellular respiration produces most of a eukaryotic cell's ATP
the vesicle that digests engulfed bacteria in all cells
questions.explain: Mitochondrion is the organelle in which aerobic cellular respiration produces most of a eukaryotic cell's ATP.
choice_explanations: This describes the Golgi apparatus, not the mitochondrion.
Prokaryotes do not have mitochondria; their DNA is in a nucleoid region.
Correct. Mitochondrion is the organelle in which aerobic cellular respiration produces most of a eukaryotic cell's ATP.
Phagolysosomes can digest engulfed material, but that is not the definition of mitochondrion.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'chloroplast'?
choices: the ATP-powered pump that moves ions across membranes
the photosynthetic organelle in plants and some protists that converts light energy into chemical energy
the structure that anchors cilia to the cell surface
the organelle that exports proteins out of the cell
questions.explain: Chloroplast is the photosynthetic organelle in plants and some protists that converts light energy into chemical energy.
choice_explanations: This describes a transport protein, not an organelle.
Correct. Chloroplast is the photosynthetic organelle in plants and some protists that converts light energy into chemical energy.
This describes basal bodies/centrioles, not chloroplasts.
This describes the secretory pathway, not a chloroplast.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'endomembrane system'?
choices: the set of organelles that all contain their own DNA
the pathway of enzymes used only in cellular respiration
the group of interacting membranes in eukaryotic cells that includes the nuclear envelope, ER, Golgi, lysosomes, vesicles, and plasma membrane
the network of protein fibers that supports cell shape
questions.explain: Endomembrane system is the group of interacting membranes in eukaryotic cells that includes the nuclear envelope, ER, Golgi, lysosomes, vesicles, and plasma membrane.
choice_explanations: This is tempting because some organelles have DNA, but many endomembrane components do not, and mitochondria/chloroplasts are generally not part of the endomembrane system.
Cellular respiration occurs mainly in mitochondria and cytosol, not in the endomembrane system.
Correct. Endomembrane system is the group of interacting membranes in eukaryotic cells that includes the nuclear envelope, ER, Golgi, lysosomes, vesicles, and plasma membrane.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'compartmentalization'?
choices: the breakdown of glucose into pyruvate
the diffusion of all molecules freely throughout the cell
the separation of cellular processes into distinct membrane-bound regions or specialized spaces
the equal distribution of chromosomes during mitosis
questions.explain: Compartmentalization is the separation of cellular processes into distinct membrane-bound regions or specialized spaces.
choice_explanations: This describes glycolysis, not the general cellular principle of compartmentalization.
Compartmentalization is the opposite of allowing all reactions to occur in one undivided space.
Correct. Compartmentalization is the separation of cellular processes into distinct membrane-bound regions or specialized spaces.
This describes cell division, not compartmentalization.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'cytoskeleton'?
choices: the collection of enzymes inside lysosomes
the phospholipid barrier surrounding the cytoplasm
the network of protein fibers that maintains cell shape, organizes components, and enables movement
the stack of membranes that modifies secretory proteins
questions.explain: Cytoskeleton is the network of protein fibers that maintains cell shape, organizes components, and enables movement.
choice_explanations: This describes lysosomal contents, not the cytoskeleton.
This describes the plasma membrane, not the cytoskeleton.
Correct. Cytoskeleton is the network of protein fibers that maintains cell shape, organizes components, and enables movement.
This describes the Golgi apparatus, not the cytoskeleton.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'microtubule'?

a membrane channel used for facilitated diffusion
an intermediate filament that resists tension
a thin actin filament used in muscle contraction and cell crawling
questions.explain: Microtubule is a hollow tubulin filament involved in cell shape, intracellular transport, cilia, flagella, and spindle formation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Microtubule is a hollow tubulin filament involved in cell shape, intracellular transport, cilia, flagella, and spindle formation.
This is a membrane transport protein, not a cytoskeletal element.
This describes intermediate filaments, not microtubules.
This describes a microfilament, not a microtubule.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'microfilament'?
choices: a membrane vesicle used in secretion
a filament specialized mainly for resisting mechanical stress
a thin actin filament involved in cell shape changes, muscle contraction, and cell movement
a hollow tube made of tubulin
questions.explain: Microfilament is a thin actin filament involved in cell shape changes, muscle contraction, and cell movement.
choice_explanations: This describes a vesicle, not a cytoskeletal filament.
This describes intermediate filaments, not microfilaments.
Correct. Microfilament is a thin actin filament involved in cell shape changes, muscle contraction, and cell movement.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'intermediate filament'?

a cytoskeletal fiber that helps reinforce cell shape and resist mechanical stress

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'cilia'?
choices: long DNA-containing organelles that make ATP
extensions of the plasma membrane used only for passive diffusion
protein channels that allow sodium ions to cross the membrane
short, numerous cell-surface projections that can move the cell or move fluid across its surface

Correct. Cilia is short, numerous cell-surface projections that can move the cell or move fluid across its surface.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'flagellum'?

a short actin projection that increases surface area for absorption
a membrane compartment that stores water
a long cell-surface appendage that propels the cell

choice_explanations: This describes a receptor, not a flagellum.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'extracellular matrix'?

a network of molecules outside animal cells that provides support, adhesion, and signaling

the carbohydrate wall surrounding bacterial cells

choice_explanations: This describes lysosomes, not extracellular matrix.
Correct. Extracellular matrix is a network of molecules outside animal cells that provides support, adhesion, and signaling.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'cell junction'?

a protein that catalyzes phospholipid synthesis
the point where chromosomes attach to spindle fibers
a specialized connection between adjacent cells that allows attachment, communication, or sealing
questions.explain: Cell junction is a specialized connection between adjacent cells that allows attachment, communication, or sealing.
choice_explanations: This is a membrane transport structure, not a junction between adjacent cells.

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'tight junction'?

a channel connecting plant cells through their walls

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'desmosome'?

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'gap junction'?

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'receptor protein'?

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'prokaryotic cell'?

questions.text: A mutation removes the signal peptide from a protein that is normally secreted from the cell. What is the most likely immediate consequence?
choices: The protein will move directly from the nucleus to the Golgi without passing through the ER.
The protein will be translated inside a lysosome because digestive organelles handle exported proteins.
The protein will automatically be secreted because all proteins leave the cell after translation.
The protein will likely remain in the cytosol instead of entering the rough ER.
questions.explain: The protein will likely remain in the cytosol instead of entering the rough ER.
choice_explanations: Proteins in the secretory pathway usually enter the rough ER first; there is no direct nucleus-to-Golgi route for a newly translated secreted protein.
Lysosomes digest materials; they are not the site where secreted proteins are translated.
Most proteins are not automatically secreted. Without the targeting signal, this protein would not be directed into the secretory pathway.
Correct. The protein will likely remain in the cytosol instead of entering the rough ER.

questions.text: A drug blocks vesicles from budding off the rough ER. What is the most likely immediate consequence?
choices: Secreted proteins will skip the Golgi and diffuse through the plasma membrane on their own.
Proteins destined for secretion will accumulate in the rough ER.
Ribosomes will stop translating all proteins immediately, including cytosolic proteins.
Mitochondria will take over packaging secreted proteins into transport vesicles.
questions.explain: Proteins destined for secretion will accumulate in the rough ER.
choice_explanations: Proteins do not simply diffuse through the plasma membrane because many are large and require vesicle trafficking.
Correct. Proteins destined for secretion will accumulate in the rough ER.
This is partly tempting because secretory proteins use rough ER-associated ribosomes, but blocking vesicle budding does not automatically stop all translation everywhere in the cell.
Mitochondria produce ATP; they do not replace the endomembrane trafficking system.

questions.text: A toxin prevents vesicles from fusing with the plasma membrane. What is the most likely immediate consequence?
choices: DNA replication will fail because secretory vesicles provide nucleotides to the nucleus.
Endocytosis will stop because membrane fusion is only required for material entering the cell.
Exocytosis of materials such as secreted proteins will decrease.
Facilitated diffusion of glucose will increase because vesicles now contain extra membrane proteins.
questions.explain: Exocytosis of materials such as secreted proteins will decrease.
choice_explanations: DNA replication does not depend on secretory vesicles supplying nucleotides in this way.
Fusion with the plasma membrane is crucial for exocytosis, not exclusively for intake. Endocytosis uses different vesicle events.
Correct. Exocytosis of materials such as secreted proteins will decrease.
Facilitated diffusion depends on transport proteins and gradients; blocking vesicle fusion does not inherently increase glucose transport.

Many membrane proteins may still be made but will be improperly processed or sorted.
The proteins will be synthesized by smooth ER instead of rough ER.

Golgi carbohydrate modification and mitochondrial DNA replication are unrelated in the way stated.

questions.text: A mutation prevents lysosomes from maintaining an acidic internal pH. What is the most likely immediate consequence?
choices: The cell wall will become stronger because acidic lysosomes normally weaken it.
Hydrolytic enzymes in lysosomes will work less effectively.

Ribosomes will add the wrong amino acids because lysosomes control translation accuracy.

Aquaporins facilitate water movement across membranes, but lysosomal acidity does not directly drive all osmosis in the cell.
Translation accuracy is determined by ribosomes, mRNA, and tRNA interactions, not by lysosomal pH.

The protein should move from nucleus to ribosome to lysosome to plasma membrane.
The protein should typically move from rough ER to Golgi to secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane.

questions.text: A mutation causes a membrane protein to fold incorrectly in the rough ER. What is the most likely immediate consequence?
choices: The protein will diffuse through the nuclear envelope because unfolded proteins cross membranes more easily.
The protein will be converted into a phospholipid in the Golgi so it can enter the membrane.
The protein may fail quality control and not proceed normally to the Golgi.
The protein will still function normally because membrane proteins do not require folding.

Proteins are not converted into phospholipids in the Golgi; those are different molecule classes.
Correct. The protein may fail quality control and not proceed normally to the Golgi.
Membrane proteins absolutely require proper folding for normal function and trafficking.

questions.text: A plant cell is engineered to produce far more cellulose-synthesizing enzymes for the plasma membrane. What is the most likely immediate consequence?
choices: The nucleolus alone should increase because all membrane proteins are assembled there.
Only mitochondria should increase because ATP is the sole requirement for placing proteins in the membrane.
The rough ER and Golgi are likely to show increased activity because many membrane proteins move through the endomembrane system.
The central vacuole should export the proteins directly to the wall without any vesicles.
questions.explain: The rough ER and Golgi are likely to show increased activity because many membrane proteins move through the endomembrane system.
choice_explanations: The nucleolus assembles ribosome subunits; it does not alone produce and route membrane proteins.
Mitochondria provide energy, but membrane protein synthesis and trafficking rely on the endomembrane system, not ATP alone.
Correct. The rough ER and Golgi are likely to show increased activity because many membrane proteins move through the endomembrane system.
The central vacuole is not the main export route for plasma-membrane proteins to the cell surface.

choices: nuclear pores will close because ER export directly powers nuclear transport.
DNA transcription will stop because the Golgi provides RNA polymerase.
cytoskeletal microfilaments will become phospholipids to replace missing vesicles.
many proteins destined for secretion or membranes will fail to reach the Golgi efficiently.

Microfilaments are protein fibers, not materials that transform into phospholipids.
Correct. many proteins destined for secretion or membranes will fail to reach the Golgi efficiently.

questions.text: Which mechanism best describes how oxygen would usually cross a cell membrane?
choices: facilitated diffusion through an aquaporin
simple diffusion through the phospholipid bilayer
active transport by an ATP-driven pump
endocytosis into a transport vesicle
questions.explain: simple diffusion through the phospholipid bilayer.
choice_explanations: Aquaporins are water channels; oxygen is small and nonpolar enough to diffuse through the bilayer directly.
Correct. simple diffusion through the phospholipid bilayer.
Pumps are used for substances moved against gradients, especially ions; oxygen usually diffuses down its gradient without ATP.
Endocytosis is for bulk uptake of large materials, not for rapid movement of small gas molecules.

simple diffusion through the phospholipid bilayer

questions.text: Which mechanism best describes how sodium ions would usually cross a cell membrane?
choices: movement through a specific channel protein or transporter
direct passage through the hydrophobic membrane core
receptor-mediated endocytosis whenever extracellular Na+ is high
simple diffusion exactly like oxygen
questions.explain: movement through a specific channel protein or transporter.
choice_explanations: Correct. movement through a specific channel protein or transporter.
Charged ions are blocked by the hydrophobic core and generally need proteins to cross efficiently.
Cells do not import sodium by receptor-mediated endocytosis as the standard route.
This is partly tempting because both involve movement across the membrane, but ions and nonpolar gases differ sharply in membrane permeability.

questions.explain: primary active transport best matches the process described.
choice_explanations: Secondary active transport uses the energy stored in another gradient rather than ATP hydrolysis directly.
Endocytosis is not the standard mechanism for pumping individual ions out of the cytosol.
Moving Ca2+ out using ATP is uphill transport, not passive facilitated diffusion.
Correct. primary active transport best matches the process described.

questions.text: A transport protein moves Ca2+ out of the cytosol using the energy of ATP hydrolysis. Which answer most precisely names the transport process?
choices: primary active transport
receptor-mediated endocytosis
secondary active transport
facilitated diffusion
questions.explain: primary active transport best matches the process described.
choice_explanations: Correct. primary active transport best matches the process described.
Endocytosis is not the standard mechanism for pumping individual ions out of the cytosol.
Secondary active transport uses the energy stored in another gradient rather than ATP hydrolysis directly.
Moving Ca2+ out using ATP is uphill transport, not passive facilitated diffusion.

questions.text: A plant cell is placed in a solution with lower solute concentration than the cytosol. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: The cell will burst immediately because the plasma membrane has no support in plants.
Water will enter the cell, and turgor pressure will increase.
There will be no net water movement because all plant cells have cell walls.
Water will leave the cell, causing plasmolysis.
questions.explain: Water will enter the cell, and turgor pressure will increase.
choice_explanations: This distractor is partly correct in noticing water entry, but plant cell walls usually prevent immediate bursting and instead create turgor pressure.
Correct. Water will enter the cell, and turgor pressure will increase.
A cell wall does not prevent osmosis; it helps the cell resist overexpansion, but net water movement can still occur.
A lower external solute concentration means the outside is hypotonic to the cell, so water does not leave; plasmolysis occurs in hypertonic solutions.

questions.text: A red blood cell is placed in a hypertonic solution. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: Water will enter the cell until it lyses.
There will be no net water movement because solutes cannot cross the membrane.
Water will leave the cell, and the cell will shrink.
The cell wall will prevent volume change.
questions.explain: Water will leave the cell, and the cell will shrink.
choice_explanations: A hypertonic solution draws water out; lysis would occur in a hypotonic environment.
Even if many solutes cannot cross quickly, water still moves osmotically in response to the solute difference.
Correct. Water will leave the cell, and the cell will shrink.
Red blood cells are animal cells and do not have cell walls.

questions.text: A paramecium moves from pond water into a much saltier solution. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: It would gain turgor pressure due to its cellulose wall.
Its contractile vacuole would likely contract less often because less water enters the cell osmotically.
Its contractile vacuole would contract more often because salt always increases water uptake.
Its plasma membrane would become completely impermeable to water.
questions.explain: Its contractile vacuole would likely contract less often because less water enters the cell osmotically.
choice_explanations: Paramecia are protists and do not have cellulose-based turgor systems like plant cells.
Correct. Its contractile vacuole would likely contract less often because less water enters the cell osmotically.
Saltier surroundings are hypertonic, so water entry decreases rather than increases.
Changing tonicity does not make a membrane absolutely impermeable to water.

questions.text: A cell with cytosol at 0.6 M total solute is placed in a solution at 0.6 M total nonpenetrating solute. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: The environment is hypertonic because nonpenetrating solutes always pull water out regardless of concentration.
Water will move equally in only one direction because isotonic means the membrane is blocked.
The environment is hypotonic because the cell contains proteins and organelles.
The environment is isotonic, so there should be no net water movement.
questions.explain: The environment is isotonic, so there should be no net water movement.
choice_explanations: Nonpenetrating solutes matter, but equal concentration does not make the outside hypertonic.
In an isotonic situation water still moves both ways; the net movement is zero, not one-way only.
Tonicity depends on the relative concentration of nonpenetrating solutes, not simply on the existence of internal proteins and organelles beyond the stated total.
Correct. The environment is isotonic, so there should be no net water movement.

questions.text: A marine alga adapted to seawater is suddenly moved to freshwater. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: Water will tend to enter the cell more rapidly because the outside becomes relatively hypotonic.
There will be no change because osmosis depends only on temperature.
Water will leave the cell because freshwater contains fewer ions.
Glucose will be pumped out to stop all water movement instantly.
questions.explain: Water will tend to enter the cell more rapidly because the outside becomes relatively hypotonic.
choice_explanations: Correct. Water will tend to enter the cell more rapidly because the outside becomes relatively hypotonic.
Temperature can affect rate, but the direction of osmosis depends on solute differences.
Fewer external ions make freshwater less concentrated, which favors water entry rather than exit.
Cells can regulate solutes, but they cannot instantly stop all water movement this way.

questions.text: A scientist wants animal cells to keep the same average volume during an experiment. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: The scientist should remove all solutes from the medium so osmosis stops.
The scientist should place them in a strongly hypotonic solution.
The scientist should place them in a strongly hypertonic solution.
The scientist should place them in an isotonic solution.

choice_explanations: Removing all solutes would create a highly hypotonic environment, not eliminate osmosis.
Hypotonic solutions make animal cells gain water and may lyse.
Hypertonic solutions make animal cells lose water and shrink.
Correct. The scientist should place them in an isotonic solution.

questions.text: A student says that a 0.8 M sucrose solution is always hypertonic to a cell. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: The statement is incorrect because tonicity is determined only by pH, not solute concentration.
The statement is correct because any solution above 0.5 M is defined as hypertonic.
The statement is correct because sucrose is a sugar and sugars always enter cells by active transport.
The statement is incomplete because tonicity depends on the cell's internal solute concentration and on whether solutes can cross.
questions.explain: The statement is incomplete because tonicity depends on the cell's internal solute concentration and on whether solutes can cross.
choice_explanations: pH can matter biologically, but tonicity is about the effect of solute differences on water movement.
There is no universal concentration cutoff that defines hypertonic without comparison to a particular cell.
Sucrose transport depends on the cell type and transporters; the claim about tonicity still requires comparison to internal conditions.
Correct. The statement is incomplete because tonicity depends on the cell's internal solute concentration and on whether solutes can cross.

questions.text: When a plant cell plasmolyzes, Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: the cell becomes isotonic to all solutions permanently.
the central vacuole swells because water enters rapidly.
the cell wall dissolves because cellulose diffuses out.
the plasma membrane pulls away from the cell wall as water leaves the cell.
questions.explain: the plasma membrane pulls away from the cell wall as water leaves the cell.
choice_explanations: Plasmolysis is a condition in a particular environment, not a permanent change making the cell isotonic to everything.
Plasmolysis happens in a hypertonic environment, so the vacuole loses water instead of swelling.
Cellulose in the wall does not simply diffuse out and dissolve during plasmolysis.
Correct. the plasma membrane pulls away from the cell wall as water leaves the cell.

questions.text: An animal cell lacking aquaporins is placed in a hypotonic solution. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: It will pump water out using ATP because osmosis always triggers active water export.
It cannot gain water at all because phospholipids are completely impermeable to water.
It may still gain water, but the rate of water movement across the membrane may be lower.
It will become a plant cell because hypotonic stress induces cell wall synthesis.
questions.explain: It may still gain water, but the rate of water movement across the membrane may be lower.
choice_explanations: Cells can regulate ions, but they do not generally pump water directly out to cancel osmosis in this way.
Water can still cross lipid bilayers slowly even without aquaporins; the key change is rate, not absolute impossibility.
Correct. It may still gain water, but the rate of water movement across the membrane may be lower.
Environmental conditions do not convert an animal cell into a plant cell.

questions.text: A cell is placed in a solution containing a solute that can freely diffuse across the membrane. Which prediction is most accurate?
choices: The solution cannot affect water movement because only proteins count as solutes in tonicity.
The solution's immediate osmolarity may matter, but its long-term tonicity can differ because the solute does not remain outside.
The solution is automatically isotonic because the solute can cross.
The solution must be hypertonic because freely diffusing solutes always pull water out permanently.
questions.explain: The solution's immediate osmolarity may matter, but its long-term tonicity can differ because the solute does not remain outside.
choice_explanations: Any dissolved particles can contribute to osmotic effects; proteins are not the only relevant solutes.
Correct. The solution's immediate osmolarity may matter, but its long-term tonicity can differ because the solute does not remain outside.
A permeable solute can reduce long-term tonicity effects, but that does not make every such solution automatically isotonic at all times.
A penetrating solute does not necessarily make a solution permanently hypertonic; its ability to cross changes the long-term water effect.

questions.topic: cell size and surface area-to-volume ratio
questions.text: a cell divides into two smaller cells of equal total volume Which statement best explains the effect on cell exchange?
choices: The combined surface area decreases because each daughter cell is smaller.
The surface area-to-volume ratio stays exactly the same because volume is conserved.
The combined surface area increases, improving exchange relative to volume.
Diffusion distance increases inside each daughter cell because there are now more membranes.
questions.explain: The combined surface area increases, improving exchange relative to volume.
choice_explanations: Smaller cells typically have a higher surface area relative to volume, not a lower one.
Volume may be conserved, but SA:V ratio is not; changing geometry changes the ratio.
Correct. The combined surface area increases, improving exchange relative to volume.
Average internal diffusion distances tend to decrease in smaller cells rather than increase.

questions.topic: cell size and surface area-to-volume ratio
questions.text: a spherical cell becomes larger without changing shape Which statement best explains the effect on cell exchange?
choices: Its surface area-to-volume ratio decreases.

Its volume decreases relative to surface area because spheres minimize area.

The absolute surface area increases, but volume increases faster than surface area, so the ratio decreases.

Spheres minimize surface area for a given volume, but a growing sphere does not end up with volume decreasing relative to area.

questions.text: the rough ER and Golgi are both membrane-bound compartments Which explanation is best?

Many secreted and membrane proteins require targeted trafficking and modification rather than simple free diffusion.
Rough ER and Golgi cooperate, but they do not perform identical roles.

questions.text: A drug prevents tubulin from polymerizing into microtubules. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Cellulose production in plant walls would cease because microtubules are made of cellulose.
Muscle contraction by actin and myosin would be the most directly blocked.
Cilia beating and chromosome movement by the spindle would be disrupted.
ATP synthesis in mitochondria would stop because tubulin lines the cristae.
questions.explain: Cilia beating and chromosome movement by the spindle would be disrupted.
choice_explanations: Microtubules are protein structures made of tubulin, not cellulose.
Actin and myosin use microfilaments, not microtubules, so this distractor confuses two cytoskeletal systems.
Correct. Cilia beating and chromosome movement by the spindle would be disrupted.
Microtubules are not structural components of mitochondrial cristae in the way stated.

questions.text: Intermediate filaments are especially important in skin cells subject to stretching. Which conclusion is best supported?

questions.text: Adjacent animal cells must form a watertight barrier to prevent leakage between them. Which conclusion is best supported?

questions.text: A sperm cell's flagellum stops beating after a microtubule motor defect. Which conclusion is best supported?

Protein secretion from the Golgi stops for the same structural reason.

questions.text: At very low temperature, an animal membrane becomes too rigid. Which statement is most accurate?

Membrane proteins affect function, but removing them is not the normal solution to low-temperature rigidity.
Correct. Cholesterol can help prevent tight packing and reduce the severity of the rigidity.
Cholesterol buffers fluidity and can reduce excessive rigidity at low temperatures rather than always increasing it.

questions.text: A phospholipid loses its hydrophilic head group. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: It would move to the nucleus to bind DNA.
It would become a stronger membrane component because the hydrophobic region is all that matters.
It would no longer behave normally as an amphipathic membrane phospholipid.
It would automatically become a membrane channel protein.
questions.explain: It would no longer behave normally as an amphipathic membrane phospholipid.
choice_explanations: There is no reason this structural change would target DNA binding in the nucleus.
Both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions are essential to phospholipid behavior in membranes.
Correct. It would no longer behave normally as an amphipathic membrane phospholipid.
A lipid does not become a protein by losing a head group.

Correct. It would no longer behave normally as an amphipathic membrane phospholipid.

Both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions are essential to phospholipid behavior in membranes.

questions.text: Researchers replace many membrane proteins with nonfunctional versions that still sit in the bilayer. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: The membrane will disappear immediately because phospholipids cannot self-assemble.
The membrane may still exist as a bilayer, but transport and signaling functions can be strongly disrupted.
Diffusion of oxygen will stop entirely because all membrane crossing requires proteins.
Only carbohydrate-based processes will change because proteins do not affect transport.
questions.explain: The membrane may still exist as a bilayer, but transport and signaling functions can be strongly disrupted.
choice_explanations: Phospholipids self-assemble into bilayers, so the membrane can still exist physically.
Correct. The membrane may still exist as a bilayer, but transport and signaling functions can be strongly disrupted.
Small nonpolar molecules like oxygen can still diffuse through the bilayer.
Membrane proteins are central to many transport and signaling functions, not irrelevant to them.

questions.text: A cell membrane suddenly becomes more permeable to ions after a pore-forming toxin inserts into it. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: The toxin likely converted carrier proteins into ribosomes.
The toxin likely disrupted the membrane's selective permeability by creating a hydrophilic path.
The toxin likely turned phospholipids into ATP, which powers ion flow.
The toxin likely increased osmosis by removing all extracellular solutes.
questions.explain: The toxin likely disrupted the membrane's selective permeability by creating a hydrophilic path.
choice_explanations: Carrier proteins and ribosomes are different structures; toxins do not explain ion leak by converting one into the other.
Correct. The toxin likely disrupted the membrane's selective permeability by creating a hydrophilic path.
Ion leakage is explained by pores, not by phospholipids becoming ATP.
Osmosis concerns water movement and would not be explained by the impossible removal of all extracellular solutes.

questions.text: A membrane protein has a mutation replacing many hydrophobic transmembrane amino acids with charged amino acids. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: The protein will automatically be secreted outside the cell without entering the ER.
The protein may insert into the membrane less effectively because the bilayer interior is hydrophobic.
The protein will become a phospholipid because of the mutation.
The protein will insert more easily because charged amino acids are attracted to lipid tails.
questions.explain: The protein may insert into the membrane less effectively because the bilayer interior is hydrophobic.
choice_explanations: Targeting and secretion are not guaranteed simply because membrane insertion is disrupted.
Correct. The protein may insert into the membrane less effectively because the bilayer interior is hydrophobic.
Amino acid changes do not convert a protein into a phospholipid.
Charged amino acids are generally unfavorable in the nonpolar membrane interior.

questions.text: A cell must increase membrane flexibility during cold adaptation. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: Increasing saturated fatty acid content is best because straight tails prevent packing.
Increasing unsaturated fatty acid content is one useful strategy.
Eliminating membrane proteins is the main way to increase flexibility.
Removing all cholesterol is always best because cholesterol only lowers fluidity.
questions.explain: Increasing unsaturated fatty acid content is one useful strategy.
choice_explanations: Straight saturated tails actually favor tight packing and reduced fluidity.
Correct. Increasing unsaturated fatty acid content is one useful strategy.
Protein removal is not the main adaptive strategy for cold-related membrane flexibility.
Cholesterol buffers fluidity; saying it only lowers fluidity is an oversimplification.

questions.text: A researcher says membrane fluidity matters only for diffusion, not for membrane proteins. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: The statement is correct because fluidity matters only in chloroplasts.
The statement is correct because proteins are fixed permanently in the fluid mosaic model.
The statement is incorrect because membranes are actually solid structures in living cells.
The statement is incorrect because membrane fluidity also affects movement and function of membrane proteins and vesicle processes.
questions.explain: The statement is incorrect because membrane fluidity also affects movement and function of membrane proteins and vesicle processes.
choice_explanations: Fluidity matters in many membranes, not only in chloroplasts.
The fluid mosaic model emphasizes that many membrane proteins can move laterally rather than being permanently fixed.
Living membranes are dynamic, not solid, but the key issue is that fluidity influences more than diffusion of small molecules.
Correct. The statement is incorrect because membrane fluidity also affects movement and function of membrane proteins and vesicle processes.

questions.text: A dialysis bag containing 20% sucrose is placed in a beaker of distilled water for 30 minutes. The membrane allows water to pass but not sucrose. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The bag's mass will stay the same because sucrose cannot cross the membrane.
The bag's mass will increase because the bag actively pumps water inward.
The bag's mass will increase because water enters the bag by osmosis.
The bag's mass will decrease because sucrose diffuses into the beaker.
questions.explain: The bag's mass will increase because water enters the bag by osmosis.
choice_explanations: The inability of sucrose to cross does not prevent water from moving; it is precisely why a net osmotic gradient is maintained.
Water movement here is passive osmosis, not active pumping by the bag.
Correct. The bag's mass will increase because water enters the bag by osmosis.
Sucrose cannot cross in this setup, and distilled water is hypotonic to the bag, so water moves into rather than sucrose moving out through the membrane.

choices: The bag's mass will decrease because sucrose diffuses into the beaker.

questions.text: A dialysis bag containing distilled water is placed in a beaker with 10% NaCl. The membrane is permeable to water but not NaCl. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The bag's mass will increase because salt pulls water into any membrane compartment.
The bag's mass will stay unchanged because NaCl cannot enter the bag.
The bag's mass will decrease because water leaves the bag by osmosis.
The bag's mass will increase because distilled water has no solutes and therefore more osmotic pressure.
questions.explain: The bag's mass will decrease because water leaves the bag by osmosis.
choice_explanations: The outside solution is hypertonic to the bag, so water leaves rather than enters.
NaCl's inability to enter does not prevent osmotic water loss; the outside remains more concentrated.
Correct. The bag's mass will decrease because water leaves the bag by osmosis.
Pure water does not have 'more osmotic pressure' than the concentrated salt solution in the way stated.

questions.text: A dialysis bag containing 5% glucose is placed in a beaker containing 5% glucose. The membrane lets water pass but not glucose. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The bag should lose mass because all solutes outside a bag are hypertonic.
The bag should gain mass because glucose inside attracts water more strongly than glucose outside.
The bag should burst because glucose is a sugar and sugars always pull in water.
There should be little or no net change in bag mass because the solutions are isotonic in this setup.
questions.explain: There should be little or no net change in bag mass because the solutions are isotonic in this setup.
choice_explanations: Tonicity depends on relative concentration, not on the solute being outside by default.
Equal nonpenetrating solute concentration on both sides means no net water movement is expected.
Sugars can contribute to osmotic gradients, but equal concentration does not create a net influx causing bursting.
Correct. There should be little or no net change in bag mass because the solutions are isotonic in this setup.

questions.text: Students place a dialysis bag with 15% starch in a beaker with iodine solution. The membrane allows iodine and water to pass but not starch. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: Starch will leave the bag and the beaker will turn blue-black instead.
Iodine will enter the bag and the contents will turn blue-black, but starch will remain inside.
Neither solution will change because the membrane blocks all molecules larger than water.
The bag will shrink because iodine is actively transported inward.
questions.explain: Iodine will enter the bag and the contents will turn blue-black, but starch will remain inside.
choice_explanations: The setup specifically states starch cannot cross, so the beaker should not become blue-black from starch leaving the bag.
Correct. Iodine will enter the bag and the contents will turn blue-black, but starch will remain inside.
Iodine is small enough to cross here, so some change should occur.
Iodine movement here is by diffusion, not active transport.

questions.text: A bag with 10% sucrose starts at 25.0 g and ends at 28.5 g after sitting in distilled water. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The data prove the bag actively transported sucrose in from the beaker.
The data support the claim that the bag interior was hypertonic to the beaker, causing net water entry.
The data show sucrose diffused out faster than water entered.
The data show the solutions were isotonic because mass changed by only a few grams.
questions.explain: The data support the claim that the bag interior was hypertonic to the beaker, causing net water entry.
choice_explanations: The beaker contained distilled water in this type of setup; active sucrose import is not what the mass increase demonstrates.
Correct. The data support the claim that the bag interior was hypertonic to the beaker, causing net water entry.
An increase in mass is consistent with water entry, not sucrose leaving.
Any clear gain in mass indicates net water movement, so isotonic is not the best interpretation.

questions.text: A dialysis bag with 0.2 M sucrose is placed in a beaker with 0.6 M sucrose. After 20 minutes, the bag mass falls. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The beaker was hypotonic to the bag because 0.6 is larger than 0.2.
The result shows water cannot move through dialysis tubing.
The bag mass fell because sucrose moved into the bag until equilibrium was reached.
The higher outside solute concentration made the beaker hypertonic to the bag.
questions.explain: The higher outside solute concentration made the beaker hypertonic to the bag.
choice_explanations: Calling the 0.6 M solution hypotonic reverses the meaning of the concentration comparison.
The observed mass change itself shows water did move.
Mass loss fits water leaving the bag, not sucrose entering it as the main cause.
Correct. The higher outside solute concentration made the beaker hypertonic to the bag.
