questions.text: In a follow-up trial, students use a membrane permeable to both water and glucose. A bag containing 10% glucose is placed in distilled water. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The bag cannot change mass because glucose permeability makes the membrane impermeable to water.
The bag will burst immediately because glucose and water always move in the same direction.
Glucose can diffuse out and water can move, so the long-term mass change may be smaller than in a membrane that traps glucose.
The bag must gain more mass because any sugar inside guarantees permanent water influx.
questions.explain: Glucose can diffuse out and water can move, so the long-term mass change may be smaller than in a membrane that traps glucose.
choice_explanations: Permeability to glucose does not imply impermeability to water.
Water and glucose do not always move in the same direction, and bursting is not guaranteed.
Correct. Glucose can diffuse out and water can move, so the long-term mass change may be smaller than in a membrane that traps glucose.
If glucose can leave, the osmotic gradient is reduced over time instead of being locked in place.

questions.text: In a follow-up trial, students use a membrane permeable to both water and glucose. A bag containing 10% glucose is placed in distilled water. What is the most likely outcome?

questions.text: A group forgets to blot water off the outside of a dialysis bag before measuring final mass. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The recorded mass decrease would be exaggerated because outside water removes mass from the balance.
The final mass would be unaffected because balances ignore external water.
The bag would become isotonic because blotting determines tonicity.
The recorded mass increase could be artificially inflated because extra surface water is included in the measurement.
questions.explain: The recorded mass increase could be artificially inflated because extra surface water is included in the measurement.
choice_explanations: Surface water would tend to add extra measured mass, not exaggerate a loss.
Balances measure whatever mass is on them, including water clinging to the outside.
Blotting affects measurement accuracy, not the actual tonicity of the solutions.
Correct. The recorded mass increase could be artificially inflated because extra surface water is included in the measurement.

questions.text: Three bags are tested for 30 minutes: Bag A contains 0.1 M sucrose, Bag B contains 0.3 M sucrose, and Bag C contains 0.6 M sucrose. All are placed in distilled water with the same size bags. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: All bags should gain the same mass because the beaker is the same for all three.
Bag C should show the greatest mass increase because it has the largest initial solute gradient relative to the beaker.
Bag C should lose mass because concentrated solutions are always hypertonic to themselves.
Bag A should show the greatest mass increase because lower internal solute always attracts more water.
questions.explain: Bag C should show the greatest mass increase because it has the largest initial solute gradient relative to the beaker.
choice_explanations: Lower internal solute creates a weaker, not stronger, gradient for water entry.
Correct. Bag C should show the greatest mass increase because it has the largest initial solute gradient relative to the beaker.
Using the same beaker conditions does not erase the different gradients created by the bag contents.
Higher internal concentration creates a stronger osmotic difference relative to distilled water.

questions.text: A bag with sucrose is placed in a beaker, and after enough time the masses stop changing even though the membrane is still intact. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: Net water movement has reached equilibrium because the opposing tendencies now balance.
Sucrose has become actively pumped out of the bag by the membrane.
Water has stopped moving entirely across the membrane.
The membrane has become a cell wall and blocked all transport.
questions.explain: Net water movement has reached equilibrium because the opposing tendencies now balance.
choice_explanations: Correct. Net water movement has reached equilibrium because the opposing tendencies now balance.
Dialysis tubing does not actively pump sucrose out.
At equilibrium, water still moves both directions; the net movement is zero rather than all movement stopping.
The membrane properties did not change into a cell wall; the system simply approached equilibrium.

questions.text: Students place equal potato cores into 0.0 M, 0.2 M, 0.4 M, 0.6 M, and 0.8 M sucrose for one hour. The 0.4 M cores show almost no change in mass. Which conclusion is most appropriate?
choices: The potato tissue is close to isotonic with 0.4 M sucrose under these conditions.
The result proves plant cells do not undergo osmosis due to their cell walls.
The potato tissue is hypotonic to all sucrose solutions because sucrose cannot enter the cells.
The 0.4 M solution caused the most osmosis because no mass change occurred.
questions.explain: The potato tissue is close to isotonic with 0.4 M sucrose under these conditions.
choice_explanations: Correct. The potato tissue is close to isotonic with 0.4 M sucrose under these conditions.
Plant cell walls do not prevent osmosis; they affect how cells respond mechanically to water movement.
The point with little or no net mass change is used to estimate the isotonic concentration, not to claim all outside solutions are hypotonic.
No net mass change suggests balanced water movement, not the greatest osmosis.

questions.text: A potato core gains 6% mass in distilled water but loses 8% mass in 0.8 M sucrose. Which conclusion is most appropriate?
choices: The cell walls pumped water out only in 0.8 M sucrose.
Sucrose entered the cells in distilled water and left in 0.8 M sucrose.
The cores synthesized extra starch in distilled water, which explains the mass gain.
Water entered the cells in distilled water and left the cells in the concentrated sucrose solution.

choice_explanations: Cell walls provide support, but they are not pumps moving water.
The pattern fits osmosis of water, not sucrose moving in distilled water where no sucrose is present.
Starch synthesis is not the simplest explanation for a rapid mass change in this osmosis lab.
Correct. Water entered the cells in distilled water and left the cells in the concentrated sucrose solution.

questions.text: One group cuts some potato cores much longer than others but still compares percent mass change across treatments. Which conclusion is most appropriate?

questions.text: After incubation, students see that potato cores in high sucrose look limp and slightly reduced in size. Which conclusion is most appropriate?
choices: The sucrose likely made the cell walls dissolve.
The mitochondria likely consumed the potato tissue during respiration.
The cells likely lost water to the hypertonic solution.
The cells likely gained turgor pressure from water influx.
questions.explain: The cells likely lost water to the hypertonic solution.
choice_explanations: Cell walls do not dissolve simply because external sucrose concentration is high.
Respiration occurs, but it does not explain the immediate osmotic shrinkage seen in this setup.
Correct. The cells likely lost water to the hypertonic solution.
Limpness in a concentrated external solution is consistent with water loss, not gain.

questions.text: A potato core in 0.5 M sucrose shows almost no mass change, but microscopy shows the cells are slightly plasmolyzed. Which conclusion is most appropriate?
choices: The data are impossible because plasmolysis can occur only when cores gain mass.
The result means sucrose was actively pumped into the cells.
The microscopy proves the balance was broken because plant cells with walls cannot plasmolyze.
The tissue may be near isotonic overall even if some individual cells still lose a small amount of water.
questions.explain: The tissue may be near isotonic overall even if some individual cells still lose a small amount of water.
choice_explanations: Plasmolysis is associated with water loss, so it is not incompatible with small net changes near isotonic conditions.
The observation concerns water balance, not evidence of active sucrose pumping.
Plant cells can plasmolyze; their plasma membrane pulls away from the wall when water is lost.
Correct. The tissue may be near isotonic overall even if some individual cells still lose a small amount of water.

questions.text: A student concludes that the highest sucrose concentration is 'best' because it caused the largest mass loss. Which conclusion is most appropriate?
choices: The conclusion is unsupported because osmosis data can never be interpreted in plant tissues.
The conclusion is supported because mass loss always means higher membrane permeability.
The conclusion is supported because plant cells function best when they are fully plasmolyzed.
That conclusion is not supported; the largest mass loss simply indicates the strongest net water movement out of the tissue in that setup.

questions.text: Red blood cells are placed in 0.9% saline and show little change in shape. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The saline is hypertonic because all salt solutions pull water out of cells.
The saline is approximately isotonic to the cells under these conditions.
The saline is hypotonic because NaCl cannot cross quickly.
The result proves the membrane is impermeable to water.
questions.explain: The saline is approximately isotonic to the cells under these conditions.
choice_explanations: Salt solutions are not automatically hypertonic; their effect depends on concentration relative to the cell.
Correct. The saline is approximately isotonic to the cells under these conditions.
The inability of a solute to cross quickly does not itself make a solution hypotonic; relative concentration matters.
Little net change in shape does not prove water cannot cross, only that net water movement is minimal.

questions.text: Cells placed in a concentrated salt solution appear crenated under the microscope. Which interpretation is best?

The cells formed cell walls in response to salt stress.
Water left the cells because the external solution was hypertonic.

Animal cells do not build cell walls in response to salt in this way.

The shape change reflects osmosis, not vesicle release.

questions.text: A student compares normal red blood cells with red blood cells treated with a poison that lowers ATP production. Both sets are then placed in isotonic saline for 10 minutes. Which interpretation is best?
choices: Only the untreated cells should shrink because ATP makes cells isotonic.
Both sets should form central vacuoles to store excess water.
Only the poisoned cells should burst immediately because ATP is always required to keep water from entering.
Both sets should remain near normal size initially because isotonic water balance does not require direct ATP-driven water pumping.
questions.explain: Both sets should remain near normal size initially because isotonic water balance does not require direct ATP-driven water pumping.
choice_explanations: Cells are not made isotonic by ATP as if ATP were a solute condition.
Red blood cells do not form central vacuoles.
ATP depletion can affect many processes, but isotonic water balance is not maintained by directly pumping water out molecule by molecule.
Correct. Both sets should remain near normal size initially because isotonic water balance does not require direct ATP-driven water pumping.

questions.text: Researchers place cultured animal cells into media with increasing sucrose concentration and count the percentage of cells that shrink. Which interpretation is best?

The percentage shrinking should stay constant because animal membranes prevent water movement.

Sucrose does not block osmosis; as a nonpenetrating solute, it can increase the osmotic gradient.
Cell shrinkage in animal cells does not require building cell walls.

questions.text: An RBC sample is moved from a hypertonic solution back into an isotonic solution. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The cells should become permanently plasmolyzed because animal cells undergo plasmolysis.
The cells should keep shrinking because water can move out but not back in.
The cells should regain a more normal volume as net water movement returns toward balance.
The cells should convert the salt outside into glucose to restore volume.

Water movement across the membrane is reversible; it is not one-way only.

Cells do not restore volume by chemically converting external salt into glucose in this way.

questions.text: A membrane-impermeable dye is added to a hypotonic solution containing red blood cells. After some cells lyse, the dye is detected inside those cells. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The dye indicates cell walls became porous.
The dye shows that hemoglobin actively transported it inward before lysis.
The dye proves intact membranes are freely permeable to all solutes in hypotonic conditions.
The dye likely entered after membrane integrity was lost during lysis, not by normal diffusion across intact membranes.

questions.text: Cells are viewed before and after adding a channel blocker that specifically prevents Na+ from crossing the plasma membrane, then placed in a hypertonic sucrose solution. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The cells should show no water movement because all osmosis requires Na+ channels.
The cells should now gain water because blocking Na+ channels reverses tonicity.
The cells should still lose water because the main osmotic gradient is created by the external sucrose, not by Na+ crossing the membrane.
The cells should undergo photosynthesis to compensate.
questions.explain: The cells should still lose water because the main osmotic gradient is created by the external sucrose, not by Na+ crossing the membrane.
choice_explanations: Water can move osmotically without Na+ channels; aquaporins or the bilayer can mediate water movement.
Tonicity depends on relative nonpenetrating solute concentration; blocking Na+ channels does not reverse a hypertonic sucrose environment.
Correct. The cells should still lose water because the main osmotic gradient is created by the external sucrose, not by Na+ crossing the membrane.
Animal cells do not perform photosynthesis.

questions.text: Scientists test two media for cultured animal cells. In medium A, cells maintain normal size. In medium B, cells swell over time. Both media contain the same total solute concentration, but in medium B one solute crosses the membrane easily. Which interpretation is best?
choices: Medium B can behave as effectively hypotonic over time because the penetrating solute reduces the long-term osmotic difference.
The result proves water cannot cross the membrane in medium A.
Medium A must contain no solutes because the cells did not change size.
Medium B must be hypertonic because equal total solute concentration always means equal tonicity.
questions.explain: Medium B can behave as effectively hypotonic over time because the penetrating solute reduces the long-term osmotic difference.
choice_explanations: Correct. Medium B can behave as effectively hypotonic over time because the penetrating solute reduces the long-term osmotic difference.
Normal cell size does not prove that water cannot cross, only that net movement is near zero.
Cells can maintain normal size in an isotonic medium that still contains solutes.
Equal total concentration does not guarantee equal tonicity if one solute can cross the membrane.

questions.text: A graph shows the average diameter of animal cells over 20 minutes after transfer to different solutions. The steepest decrease occurs in 1.0 M sucrose. Which interpretation is best?
choices: That treatment had the lowest osmotic pressure because the cells shrank fastest.
That treatment made cell walls contract around the cells.
That treatment produced the strongest active transport of sucrose into the cells.
That treatment produced the strongest net water loss from the cells in the time measured.
questions.explain: That treatment produced the strongest net water loss from the cells in the time measured.
choice_explanations: A stronger external sucrose concentration corresponds to a stronger osmotic pull on water, not lower osmotic pressure in the sense implied.
Animal cells do not have cell walls.
The diameter decrease reflects water leaving, not active sucrose import.

questions.text: Students place agar cubes containing a pH indicator into acid. After 10 minutes, the outer 2 mm of each cube changes color. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The largest cubes will have the greatest percentage changed because they have the greatest total surface area.
The smallest cubes will have the greatest percentage of their volume changed because the same diffusion depth reaches a larger fraction of the cube.
The smallest cubes will have the smallest percentage changed because they contain less agar.
All cubes will have the same percentage changed because the acid diffused the same distance into each.
questions.explain: The smallest cubes will have the greatest percentage of their volume changed because the same diffusion depth reaches a larger fraction of the cube.
choice_explanations: Greater total surface area does not mean a greater fraction of the entire volume is penetrated.
Correct. The smallest cubes will have the greatest percentage of their volume changed because the same diffusion depth reaches a larger fraction of the cube.
Having less agar does not reduce the fraction penetrated when the diffusion distance is the same.
Equal diffusion depth does not translate into equal percentage of volume affected when cube sizes differ.

questions.text: Three agar cubes have side lengths of 1 cm, 2 cm, and 3 cm. After equal time in acid, only the center of the 3 cm cube remains pink. Which conclusion is best supported?

choice_explanations: The observation reflects slower complete penetration, not an absolute size cutoff preventing diffusion.

Indicator concentration per unit volume is not the main explanation when the same agar preparation is used.

questions.text: A class compares cubes with the same total volume but different shapes: one compact block and one thin sheet-like shape. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The thin sheet-like shape should be penetrated more completely because it exposes more surface area relative to volume.
The thin sheet should diffuse more slowly because it contains less internal space.
The compact block should be penetrated more completely because thick shapes attract more acid.
Both should be identical because only total volume matters for diffusion.
questions.explain: The thin sheet-like shape should be penetrated more completely because it exposes more surface area relative to volume.
choice_explanations: Correct. The thin sheet-like shape should be penetrated more completely because it exposes more surface area relative to volume.
Reduced thickness shortens diffusion distance and tends to speed complete penetration.
Acid diffusion is not driven by a 'thick shape' attraction; geometry matters through surface area and distance.
Total volume alone does not determine diffusion efficiency; shape matters too.

questions.text: Students accidentally leave one set of agar cubes in acid for twice as long as another otherwise identical set. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The longer-incubated set should show greater penetration because diffusion continues over time.
Time matters only for living cells, not for diffusion in agar.
The longer-incubated set should have less penetration because the acid is used up by the surface layer immediately.

questions.explain: The longer-incubated set should show greater penetration because diffusion continues over time.
choice_explanations: Correct. The longer-incubated set should show greater penetration because diffusion continues over time.
Diffusion in nonliving agar still depends strongly on time.
Acid is not instantly exhausted at the surface in this typical setup.
Cube size matters, but time also matters because molecules keep diffusing as long as a gradient remains.

questions.text: A teacher asks why one-celled organisms are usually small and the agar cube lab is used as a model. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Smaller cells, like smaller cubes, generally exchange materials more efficiently because more of their volume lies close to the surface.
Smaller cells are used because only small cells can contain enzymes.
Smaller cubes prove that diffusion is an active transport process.
The model shows that large cells have no plasma membranes.
questions.explain: Smaller cells, like smaller cubes, generally exchange materials more efficiently because more of their volume lies close to the surface.
choice_explanations: Correct. Smaller cells, like smaller cubes, generally exchange materials more efficiently because more of their volume lies close to the surface.
Large cells can also contain enzymes; the issue modeled here is exchange efficiency.
Diffusion is a passive process, not active transport.
Cell size has nothing to do with whether a plasma membrane is present.

questions.text: A graph shows that acid penetrated 0.3 cm into all cube sizes after the same time. The 1 cm cube is completely penetrated, but the 2 cm cube is not. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The 2 cm cube contains more acid-neutralizing proteins by definition.
The result proves the 2 cm cube has lower total surface area than the 1 cm cube.
The 1 cm cube has a shorter distance from surface to center, so the same penetration depth reaches all the way through.
The 1 cm cube must have had a different membrane that allowed faster diffusion.
questions.explain: The 1 cm cube has a shorter distance from surface to center, so the same penetration depth reaches all the way through.
choice_explanations: The cubes are model systems and do not contain proteins by definition in this lab.
The 2 cm cube has greater total surface area, but lower surface area relative to volume.

The material is the same agar; the key difference is geometry, not a different membrane.

questions.text: Students calculate surface area-to-volume ratio for cube models before diffusion. The 1 cm cube has the highest ratio. Which conclusion is best supported?

Solute identity matters, but geometry still affects how efficiently the whole object exchanges.
High surface area-to-volume ratio means the opposite: more area available relative to the volume being supplied.

questions.text: A student argues that a larger cube is always better because it can store more nutrients. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: That may be partly true, but the cube model shows a tradeoff: larger size lowers surface area relative to volume and can slow exchange.

The argument is unrelated because cells never face exchange limits.

choice_explanations: Correct. That may be partly true, but the cube model shows a tradeoff: larger size lowers surface area relative to volume and can slow exchange.

Larger cubes actually do have more total surface area, just not more relative surface area.
Cells absolutely do face exchange limits, which is why the model is used.

Lower temperature likely slowed molecular motion and diffusion rate.
Lower temperature increased surface area-to-volume ratio.

The direction of the gradient is set by concentration difference, not by temperature alone.

questions.text: A student cuts a 3 cm cube into twenty-seven 1 cm cubes before placing them in acid. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The smaller cubes together will have less total surface area because cutting removes outer layers.
The smaller cubes together will have a greater total surface area than the original intact cube, improving overall exposure to the acid.
The total volume will increase because more cubes means more matter.
The smaller cubes will each have lower surface area-to-volume ratio than the original cube.
questions.explain: The smaller cubes together will have a greater total surface area than the original intact cube, improving overall exposure to the acid.
choice_explanations: Cutting creates new exposed surfaces rather than removing all useful surface.
Correct. The smaller cubes together will have a greater total surface area than the original intact cube, improving overall exposure to the acid.
Cutting redistributes the same material and does not increase total volume.
Smaller cubes have higher, not lower, surface area-to-volume ratios.

questions.text: Scientists add a brief pulse of radioactive amino acids to pancreatic cells, then track label location over time. Label first appears in rough ER, then Golgi, then secretory vesicles. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The data support the pathway rough ER → Golgi → vesicles for a secreted protein.
The data support the pathway nucleus → lysosome → plasma membrane.
The data show proteins diffuse randomly through the cytosol before secretion.
The data prove secreted proteins are synthesized in the Golgi.
questions.explain: The data support the pathway rough ER → Golgi → vesicles for a secreted protein.
choice_explanations: Correct. The data support the pathway rough ER → Golgi → vesicles for a secreted protein.
The observed sequence does not fit the nucleus-to-lysosome route for secreted proteins.
The ordered movement through organelles argues against random diffusion as the main pathway.
The first appearance in rough ER argues against Golgi synthesis of the proteins.

questions.text: In a similar pulse-chase experiment, a drug blocks vesicle budding from the ER. Radioactive label accumulates in the rough ER and is reduced in the Golgi. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The labeled amino acids could no longer enter the nucleus.
The Golgi was converted into a lysosome.
Translation in ribosomes was completely halted before proteins could enter the ER.
Transport from the ER to the Golgi was disrupted.
questions.explain: Transport from the ER to the Golgi was disrupted.
choice_explanations: The key defect concerns ER-to-Golgi trafficking, not amino acid entry into the nucleus.
Golgi-to-lysosome conversion is not what the label distribution indicates.
Proteins still accumulated in the rough ER, so translation of these proteins was not fully halted.
Correct. Transport from the ER to the Golgi was disrupted.

questions.text: Researchers mutate the signal peptide of a normally secreted enzyme and then perform fluorescence microscopy. Most fluorescence remains in the cytosol. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The altered protein was probably secreted faster because it skipped the ER.
The altered protein likely entered the chloroplast instead.
The altered protein likely failed to enter the rough ER-targeted secretory pathway.
The altered protein was translated by lysosomes and trapped there.
questions.explain: The altered protein likely failed to enter the rough ER-targeted secretory pathway.
choice_explanations: Skipping ER targeting does not make a secreted protein automatically export faster; it usually mislocalizes it.
There is no reason in the setup to infer chloroplast targeting.
Correct. The altered protein likely failed to enter the rough ER-targeted secretory pathway.
Lysosomes are not sites of translation.

questions.text: Researchers mutate the signal peptide of a normally secreted enzyme and then perform fluorescence microscopy. Most fluorescence remains in the cytosol. Which answer best explains the results?
choices: The altered protein likely failed to enter the rough ER-targeted secretory pathway.
The altered protein likely entered the chloroplast instead.
The altered protein was probably secreted faster because it skipped the ER.
The altered protein was translated by lysosomes and trapped there.
questions.explain: The altered protein likely failed to enter the rough ER-targeted secretory pathway.
choice_explanations: Correct. The altered protein likely failed to enter the rough ER-targeted secretory pathway.
There is no reason in the setup to infer chloroplast targeting.
Skipping ER targeting does not make a secreted protein automatically export faster; it usually mislocalizes it.
Lysosomes are not sites of translation.

questions.text: A toxin prevents vesicles from fusing with the plasma membrane. A fluorescent secretory protein accumulates in vesicles near the cell surface. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The protein was converted into membrane lipid near the surface.
The nucleus stopped transcribing the protein as soon as vesicles formed.
Endocytosis was blocked before the protein entered the rough ER.
Exocytosis was blocked after vesicles formed and moved outward.
questions.explain: Exocytosis was blocked after vesicles formed and moved outward.
choice_explanations: Proteins are not converted into lipids in this way.
Transcription defects would not specifically cause accumulation in peripheral vesicles after synthesis.
The accumulation of filled vesicles near the membrane points to a late secretory defect, not early endocytosis.
Correct. Exocytosis was blocked after vesicles formed and moved outward.

questions.text: Cells making a membrane receptor are given labeled amino acids. The label appears in rough ER, then Golgi, then the plasma membrane. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The receptor first enters lysosomes so it can be folded.
The receptor is synthesized directly inside the plasma membrane and never enters organelles.
The receptor is made in mitochondria because membrane proteins require ATP.
The receptor follows the endomembrane pathway before insertion into the membrane.
questions.explain: The receptor follows the endomembrane pathway before insertion into the membrane.
choice_explanations: Lysosomes digest materials and are not the normal first destination for folding new membrane receptors.
Membrane proteins are not synthesized directly inside the plasma membrane.
ATP use does not mean mitochondria synthesize the receptor.
Correct. The receptor follows the endomembrane pathway before insertion into the membrane.

questions.text: A student measures the amount of a tagged secreted protein in the rough ER and Golgi every 10 minutes. ER signal peaks first, then declines as Golgi signal rises. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The protein must be synthesized in the nucleus because the ER signal changed first.
The protein is being trafficked from ER to Golgi over time.
The protein is moving from Golgi back to ER because the ER signal was highest first.
The protein is diffusing out of the cell directly from the ER lumen.
questions.explain: The protein is being trafficked from ER to Golgi over time.
choice_explanations: The nucleus contains the gene, but protein synthesis for secreted proteins occurs on rough-ER-associated ribosomes.
Correct. The protein is being trafficked from ER to Golgi over time.
An early ER peak followed by a Golgi rise is exactly what would be expected for forward trafficking, not reverse movement.
Secreted proteins do not normally diffuse straight out from the ER lumen to the outside of the cell.

questions.text: Researchers block Golgi glycosylation enzymes and find that a tagged membrane protein reaches the membrane much less efficiently. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The result proves membrane proteins are synthesized in the Golgi.
The result shows that phospholipids, not proteins, determine membrane localization.
The membrane protein could not be transcribed because glycosylation occurs before mRNA is made.
Golgi processing likely helps the protein fold correctly or be sorted to its final destination.
questions.explain: Golgi processing likely helps the protein fold correctly or be sorted to its final destination.
choice_explanations: Reduced membrane delivery does not show the protein was synthesized in the Golgi.
Lipids matter, but the experiment specifically implicates processing of the protein itself.
Glycosylation in the Golgi affects protein processing after transcription, not before mRNA synthesis.
Correct. Golgi processing likely helps the protein fold correctly or be sorted to its final destination.

questions.text: A fluorescent secretory protein is followed in cells treated with brefeldin-like disruption of Golgi organization. Signal remains near the ER and secretion falls. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The treatment proves secreted proteins are made by smooth ER.
Disrupting Golgi function interfered with normal trafficking and secretion of the protein.
The treatment only affected mitochondrial proteins, not secreted proteins.
The treatment increased simple diffusion of the protein across the plasma membrane.
questions.explain: Disrupting Golgi function interfered with normal trafficking and secretion of the protein.
choice_explanations: Secreted proteins are synthesized on ribosomes associated with rough ER, not smooth ER.
Correct. Disrupting Golgi function interfered with normal trafficking and secretion of the protein.
The described signal pattern specifically involves a secretory protein and Golgi function.
Secretion fell rather than rose, so increased diffusion across the plasma membrane is not supported.

questions.text: Scientists fluorescently tag a lysosomal enzyme. In untreated cells the signal ends up in lysosomes, but in mutant cells it is secreted outside the cell. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The mutation likely converted the enzyme into a phospholipid.
The mutation likely made lysosomes start translating proteins.
The mutation likely disrupted sorting information that normally directs the enzyme from the Golgi to lysosomes.
The mutation likely destroyed all ribosomes in the mutant cells.
questions.explain: The mutation likely disrupted sorting information that normally directs the enzyme from the Golgi to lysosomes.
choice_explanations: The experiment concerns localization of an enzyme, not chemical conversion into a lipid.
Lysosomes are not translation sites, and the issue here is mis-sorting after synthesis.
Correct. The mutation likely disrupted sorting information that normally directs the enzyme from the Golgi to lysosomes.
The cells are still making the enzyme, so all ribosomes were not destroyed.

questions.text: A researcher tracks a secreted protein after adding a microtubule inhibitor. The protein is synthesized normally but accumulates in scattered vesicles and is secreted more slowly. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Microtubules must form the protein's peptide bonds.
The rough ER must be made entirely of microtubules.
The protein slowed because oxygen could no longer diffuse through the membrane.
Microtubule-dependent vesicle transport likely contributes to efficient movement of secretory vesicles.
questions.explain: Microtubule-dependent vesicle transport likely contributes to efficient movement of secretory vesicles.
choice_explanations: Protein synthesis itself occurs on ribosomes, not by microtubules making peptide bonds.
Rough ER is a membrane network studded with ribosomes, not a microtubule organelle.
The specific defect involves vesicle distribution and secretion, not oxygen diffusion.
Correct. Microtubule-dependent vesicle transport likely contributes to efficient movement of secretory vesicles.

questions.text: Cells are placed in a solution containing a fluorescent glucose analog. At room temperature the analog enters quickly, but entry falls sharply when the membrane glucose carrier is blocked. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The data show the analog is synthesized inside the cell and not imported.
The data show glucose analog uptake requires vesicle fusion with lysosomes.
The data support carrier-mediated transport of the glucose analog.
The data prove the analog enters only by simple diffusion through phospholipids.
questions.explain: The data support carrier-mediated transport of the glucose analog.
choice_explanations: Rapid external-to-internal labeling supports import, not internal synthesis.
Lysosomal vesicle fusion is not the relevant mechanism for uptake of a small glucose analog in this setup.
Correct. The data support carrier-mediated transport of the glucose analog.
A specific blocker reducing uptake argues against simple diffusion as the main mechanism.

questions.text: Researchers deplete ATP in root cells and measure H+ pumping across the plasma membrane. H+ export decreases greatly. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The H+ pump is part of receptor-mediated endocytosis.
The H+ export proves water cannot cross the membrane.
The H+ pump is carrying out simple diffusion, so ATP should not matter.
The H+ pump is likely using ATP directly for active transport.

choice_explanations: Pumping H+ across the membrane is not receptor-mediated endocytosis.

A strong ATP dependence is inconsistent with simple diffusion.

questions.text: A Na+-dependent cotransporter moves amino acids into animal cells. When the Na+ gradient is experimentally collapsed, amino acid uptake falls even though ATP is still present. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The transporter must be a simple diffusion channel for amino acids.
The transporter relies on the Na+ gradient as an energy source for secondary active transport.
The result shows amino acids normally enter by phagocytosis.
The transporter requires direct ATP hydrolysis at the amino acid-binding site.
questions.explain: The transporter relies on the Na+ gradient as an energy source for secondary active transport.
choice_explanations: A collapse in uptake when the Na+ gradient is lost is inconsistent with simple diffusion.
Correct. The transporter relies on the Na+ gradient as an energy source for secondary active transport.
Amino acid uptake in this context is not explained by phagocytosis.
ATP may help maintain the Na+ gradient elsewhere, but the result points to coupling to the gradient rather than direct ATP hydrolysis by this transporter.

questions.text: An aquaporin inhibitor is added to plant cells placed in a hypotonic solution. The cells still gain water but do so more slowly. Which interpretation is best supported?

The result proves aquaporins are ATP-powered water pumps.
Aquaporins increase the rate of water movement, but water can still cross membranes without them.
The result shows the inhibitor destroyed the cell wall.

choice_explanations: Direction of osmosis depends on the water potential gradient, not on aquaporins themselves.

The observation concerns membrane water permeability, not cell wall destruction.

questions.text: A chloride channel opens and chloride ions move into the cell down their electrochemical gradient. Channel blockers sharply reduce entry. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The experiment supports facilitated diffusion through a channel protein.

The experiment supports simple diffusion of ions through the bilayer.
The experiment supports primary active transport because ions moved across the membrane.
questions.explain: The experiment supports facilitated diffusion through a channel protein.
choice_explanations: Correct. The experiment supports facilitated diffusion through a channel protein.
Pinocytosis involves vesicles and fluid uptake, not the passage of ions through a channel.
Charged ions generally require proteins rather than crossing the hydrophobic bilayer efficiently by simple diffusion.
Movement down a gradient through a channel is passive, not ATP-driven primary active transport.

questions.text: A researcher measures uptake of a dye-labeled LDL particle by cultured cells. Uptake drops when membrane receptors are blocked. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The LDL is likely entering by receptor-mediated endocytosis.

choice_explanations: Correct. The LDL is likely entering by receptor-mediated endocytosis.

Aquaporins are water channels, not LDL entry pathways.
Osmosis refers to water movement, not uptake of lipid-protein particles.

choices: The LDL is likely entering by receptor-mediated endocytosis.
The LDL is entering by osmosis because it is dissolved in water.
The LDL is entering by simple diffusion because all lipids cross membranes directly.
The LDL is entering through aquaporins because it contains cholesterol.
questions.explain: The LDL is likely entering by receptor-mediated endocytosis.
choice_explanations: Correct. The LDL is likely entering by receptor-mediated endocytosis.
Osmosis refers to water movement, not uptake of lipid-protein particles.
Large LDL particles do not simply diffuse through the bilayer like small nonpolar molecules.
Aquaporins are water channels, not LDL entry pathways.

questions.text: A proton pump is inhibited in a membrane system that normally drives sucrose uptake through a cotransporter. Sucrose uptake then falls. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The proton gradient generated by the pump likely powers the cotransporter indirectly.
The cotransporter is actually a lysosome because it depends on pH.
Sucrose normally enters by simple diffusion, so the result is unrelated.
The proton pump must be making sucrose in the membrane.
questions.explain: The proton gradient generated by the pump likely powers the cotransporter indirectly.
choice_explanations: Correct. The proton gradient generated by the pump likely powers the cotransporter indirectly.
A cotransporter is a transport protein, not an organelle.
Dependence on the proton pump is inconsistent with simple diffusion as the main route.
Membrane pumps move ions; they do not synthesize sucrose.

questions.text: At 4°C, uptake of a fluorescent phospholipid vesicle by cultured cells drops much more than uptake of oxygen gas. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The data show oxygen cannot cross membranes at room temperature.
The data prove vesicles and oxygen use the same membrane channel.
Oxygen enters by active transport, so it is less affected by low temperature.
Vesicle-mediated uptake is more temperature-sensitive and complex than simple diffusion of small nonpolar gases.
questions.explain: Vesicle-mediated uptake is more temperature-sensitive and complex than simple diffusion of small nonpolar gases.
choice_explanations: The experiment says oxygen uptake still occurs; it does not imply inability at room temperature.
The very different temperature responses argue against a shared single channel mechanism.
Oxygen typically crosses by simple diffusion, not active transport.
Correct. Vesicle-mediated uptake is more temperature-sensitive and complex than simple diffusion of small nonpolar gases.

questions.text: Researchers compare a normal cell line with one missing a specific glucose transporter. In equal external glucose, the mutant takes up far less glucose unless glucose is made extremely concentrated. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The mutant lacks all phospholipids, which explains the result.
The data prove glucose is nonpolar and crosses mostly by simple diffusion.
The transporter normally prevents glucose entry, so removing it should raise uptake.
The transporter normally mediates most glucose entry, though very high concentrations may allow some limited movement by other routes.
questions.explain: The transporter normally mediates most glucose entry, though very high concentrations may allow some limited movement by other routes.
choice_explanations: The result does not suggest loss of the membrane bilayer itself.
Residual uptake at extreme concentration does not mean glucose usually crosses mainly by simple diffusion.
Lower uptake in the mutant shows the transporter promotes entry rather than blocks it.
Correct. The transporter normally mediates most glucose entry, though very high concentrations may allow some limited movement by other routes.

questions.text: A toxin forms nonspecific pores in the plasma membrane. Cells rapidly lose ion gradients and then die. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The toxin kills cells because pores convert ATP into ions.
The toxin proves ion gradients are maintained only by the cell wall.
The toxin disrupts selective permeability, a core function of the plasma membrane.
The toxin improves transport efficiency by letting every solute move freely.
questions.explain: The toxin disrupts selective permeability, a core function of the plasma membrane.
choice_explanations: Pores do not convert ATP into ions; they allow uncontrolled leakage.
Animal cells lack cell walls, and ion gradients are maintained by membranes and transport proteins.
Correct. The toxin disrupts selective permeability, a core function of the plasma membrane.
Free movement of all solutes destroys controlled homeostasis rather than improving transport.

questions.text: Scientists make artificial membranes from two lipid mixtures. Membrane A has more saturated fatty acids; Membrane B has more unsaturated fatty acids. At low temperature, Membrane B allows embedded proteins to keep functioning better. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: The unsaturated tails in Membrane B likely help maintain fluidity at low temperature.
The unsaturated tails in Membrane B likely make the membrane more rigid by packing tightly.
Membrane B must contain more ATP, which keeps proteins active.
The proteins function better only because unsaturated tails are carbohydrates.
questions.explain: The unsaturated tails in Membrane B likely help maintain fluidity at low temperature.
choice_explanations: Correct. The unsaturated tails in Membrane B likely help maintain fluidity at low temperature.
Unsaturated tails introduce kinks that reduce tight packing rather than increasing it.
Lipid composition affects fluidity, but membranes do not contain ATP in the way suggested as the explanation.
Unsaturated tails are features of fatty acids in lipids, not carbohydrates.

questions.text: Researchers measure dye leakage from liposomes at 40°C. Liposomes with more cholesterol leak less dye than liposomes without cholesterol. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: Cholesterol likely reduced excessive membrane fluidity and permeability at the higher temperature.
Cholesterol must have made the membrane a rigid cell wall.
Cholesterol prevented leakage by digesting dye molecules.
Cholesterol likely served as an ATP-powered pump that moved dye back in.
questions.explain: Cholesterol likely reduced excessive membrane fluidity and permeability at the higher temperature.
choice_explanations: Correct. Cholesterol likely reduced excessive membrane fluidity and permeability at the higher temperature.
Membranes with cholesterol remain membranes, not cell walls.
Cholesterol does not digest molecules; the effect is structural.
Cholesterol is a lipid component, not a pump protein.

questions.text: A membrane protein works best when the surrounding membrane is fluid enough for the protein to change shape. At low temperature, activity falls but is partly restored when more unsaturated phospholipids are added. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: Protein function depends in part on membrane fluidity, which was improved by the unsaturated lipids.
Unsaturated lipids restored activity by converting the protein into a carbohydrate.
Protein function depends only on amino acid sequence and cannot be influenced by the membrane.
The added unsaturated lipids increased cell wall thickness around the protein.
questions.explain: Protein function depends in part on membrane fluidity, which was improved by the unsaturated lipids.
choice_explanations: Correct. Protein function depends in part on membrane fluidity, which was improved by the unsaturated lipids.
Lipids do not convert proteins into carbohydrates.
A protein's sequence matters, but its membrane environment can also affect conformation and activity.
This is a membrane experiment, not a cell wall-thickening phenomenon.

questions.text: A lab compares permeability of two membranes to small ions. Membrane X contains a pore-forming toxin; Membrane Y does not. Ion gradients collapse only in Membrane X. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: The toxin turned the bilayer into DNA.
The pore-forming toxin disrupted selective permeability by creating a hydrophilic path for ions.
The toxin changed osmolarity only by removing all water from the membrane.
The toxin improved facilitated diffusion specificity.

choice_explanations: Membranes do not transform into DNA under these conditions.
Correct. The pore-forming toxin disrupted selective permeability by creating a hydrophilic path for ions.
The result is best explained by ion leak through pores, not by water removal.
A nonspecific pore lowers, rather than improves, selectivity.

questions.text: Cells grown at colder temperatures are found to produce more unsaturated membrane lipids than cells grown warm. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: The change likely helps keep the membrane from becoming too rigid in the cold.
The change likely creates a cell wall in cells that lack one.
The change likely prevents all membrane proteins from moving.
The change likely stops osmosis by eliminating hydrophilic heads.
questions.explain: The change likely helps keep the membrane from becoming too rigid in the cold.
choice_explanations: Correct. The change likely helps keep the membrane from becoming too rigid in the cold.
Lipid remodeling does not create a cell wall.
More unsaturated tails tend to increase, not freeze, membrane flexibility.
The hydrophilic heads are still present; the experiment concerns tail saturation.

questions.text: Two membrane samples are tested: one contains mostly saturated phospholipids, the other mostly unsaturated phospholipids. At the same temperature, the saturated sample resists deformation more. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: The result proves only proteins determine membrane behavior.
The saturated tails likely pack more tightly, reducing fluidity.
The saturated sample must contain more ribosomes between the lipids.
The saturated sample is more fluid because straight tails cannot approach each other.

choice_explanations: Lipids themselves strongly influence membrane properties.

Ribosomes are not inserted between phospholipid tails as the main explanation here.
Straight saturated tails pack more tightly, not less tightly.

questions.text: Fluorescently labeled membrane proteins are tracked over time. In a normal membrane they move laterally, but in a chemically cross-linked membrane they move much less. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: The data prove membranes are solid crystals in cells.
The data show proteins are permanently fixed in all living membranes.
The data show phospholipids are absent from the normal membrane.
The data support the fluid mosaic model, in which many membrane components can move within the bilayer.
questions.explain: The data support the fluid mosaic model, in which many membrane components can move within the bilayer.
choice_explanations: Living membranes are dynamic rather than crystal-like.
Reduced movement after cross-linking implies that movement normally occurs; it does not support permanent immobility.
Protein movement does not imply absence of phospholipids; the bilayer is still present.
Correct. The data support the fluid mosaic model, in which many membrane components can move within the bilayer.

questions.text: A detergent is added at low concentration and causes membrane proteins to dissociate from the bilayer. Transport drops sharply. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: Proper membrane organization is required for many transport proteins to function.
Transport drops because all diffusion requires ATP.
Transport drops because detergents stop transcription in the nucleus first.
Transport drops because the detergent turned channels into lysosomes.
questions.explain: Proper membrane organization is required for many transport proteins to function.
choice_explanations: Correct. Proper membrane organization is required for many transport proteins to function.
Passive diffusion does not require ATP, though many membrane proteins do require intact structure.
The immediate effect is on membrane structure, not first on transcription.
Channels are proteins; lysosomes are organelles, so this conversion claim is nonsensical.

choice_explanations: The immediate effect is on membrane structure, not first on transcription.
Channels are proteins; lysosomes are organelles, so this conversion claim is nonsensical.
Passive diffusion does not require ATP, though many membrane proteins do require intact structure.
Correct. Proper membrane organization is required for many transport proteins to function.

questions.text: A mutation removes cholesterol-binding sites from a membrane protein that normally works best in certain lipid regions. The protein now functions poorly despite being present in normal amounts. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: Protein amount is all that matters, so function should be unchanged.
The result proves membranes are made only of cholesterol.
The mutation must have changed the protein into a phospholipid.
The surrounding membrane environment can influence protein function even when the protein is present.
questions.explain: The surrounding membrane environment can influence protein function even when the protein is present.
choice_explanations: Presence alone does not guarantee normal function if the membrane environment or localization is altered.
Membranes are composed mainly of phospholipids with proteins and other components, not cholesterol alone.
A protein mutation does not convert the protein into a lipid.
Correct. The surrounding membrane environment can influence protein function even when the protein is present.

questions.text: Scientists isolate lysosomes from animal cells and raise the internal pH from acidic to nearly neutral. Breakdown of engulfed material slows greatly. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The result shows lysosomes are actually mitochondria.
Many lysosomal enzymes require an acidic environment to function efficiently.
The result proves lysosomes synthesize the digestive enzymes from amino acids in the lumen.
Lysosomes digest best at neutral pH because they are continuous with the cytosol.
questions.explain: Many lysosomal enzymes require an acidic environment to function efficiently.
choice_explanations: A pH-dependent digestive compartment is not thereby a mitochondrion.
Correct. Many lysosomal enzymes require an acidic environment to function efficiently.
Reduced digestion at altered pH says nothing about where the enzymes were synthesized.
Lysosomes maintain a distinct acidic lumen rather than simply matching cytosolic pH.

questions.text: A fluorescent dye that accumulates in acidic compartments is used on normal cells and cells treated with a proton-pump inhibitor. Lysosomes in treated cells fluoresce much less. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The inhibitor likely increased protein synthesis in the rough ER.
The inhibitor likely increased osmosis into lysosomes because pumps move water.
The inhibitor likely turned lysosomes into chloroplasts.
The inhibitor likely reduced acidification of lysosomes by blocking proton transport.
questions.explain: The inhibitor likely reduced acidification of lysosomes by blocking proton transport.
choice_explanations: The dye reports acidic compartments, so rough-ER protein synthesis is not what the fluorescence change directly measures.
Proton pumps move ions, not water directly.
Lower acidity does not imply conversion into chloroplasts.
Correct. The inhibitor likely reduced acidification of lysosomes by blocking proton transport.

questions.text: Plant cells are transferred from water to a concentrated salt solution. The central vacuole becomes smaller and the plasma membrane pulls away from the wall. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Water left the vacuole and cytoplasm because the external solution was hypertonic.
The chloroplasts actively pumped water out of the wall space.
The cell wall dissolved, causing the membrane to detach.
Water entered the vacuole because salts always pull water into plant cells.
questions.explain: Water left the vacuole and cytoplasm because the external solution was hypertonic.
choice_explanations: Correct. Water left the vacuole and cytoplasm because the external solution was hypertonic.
Chloroplasts are not the direct explanation for the osmotic response described.
The observed detachment is plasmolysis, not dissolution of the wall.
A concentrated external solution causes water loss, not gain, from the plant cell.

questions.text: Researchers compare plant cells with normal vacuoles to mutant cells with very small vacuoles. In dilute conditions, the mutant cells become less rigid. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The result shows vacuoles synthesize cellulose for the wall.
The smaller vacuoles must have increased ATP production instead.
Rigid cell walls alone make vacuoles unnecessary for plant support.
Large central vacuoles contribute to turgor pressure and help maintain rigidity in plant cells.
questions.explain: Large central vacuoles contribute to turgor pressure and help maintain rigidity in plant cells.
choice_explanations: Vacuoles store materials and affect water balance; they are not the site of cellulose synthesis.
ATP production is primarily a mitochondrial role and does not explain the rigidity change directly.
Cell walls provide support, but vacuole-driven turgor pressure is also important for rigidity.
Correct. Large central vacuoles contribute to turgor pressure and help maintain rigidity in plant cells.

questions.text: In liver cells, a drug that disrupts smooth ER function leads to poorer detoxification of a chemical compound. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The nucleolus must export the detox enzymes directly into the blood.
The smooth ER likely contains enzymes involved in detoxifying that compound.
The rough ER must be the main detox organelle because it has ribosomes.
The Golgi must be the only membrane system involved in chemical processing.
questions.explain: The smooth ER likely contains enzymes involved in detoxifying that compound.
choice_explanations: The nucleolus assembles ribosomal components and does not export detox enzymes directly to the blood.
Correct. The smooth ER likely contains enzymes involved in detoxifying that compound.
Rough ER focuses on protein synthesis for many membrane/secreted proteins, whereas smooth ER is classically associated with detoxification.
The Golgi is important for sorting and modification, but the disrupted detoxification specifically points to smooth ER function.

questions.text: Scientists label newly synthesized membrane lipids and watch them first accumulate in smooth ER before appearing in other membranes. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The rough ER must have synthesized the lipids because all ER regions are identical in function.
The nucleus must have made the lipids because it controls the cell.
The plasma membrane must have synthesized the lipids and sent them backward to the ER.
The smooth ER is a major site of lipid synthesis for the endomembrane system.
questions.explain: The smooth ER is a major site of lipid synthesis for the endomembrane system.
choice_explanations: The ER is continuous, but rough and smooth regions are not functionally identical.
Controlling the cell genetically is different from being the major site of lipid synthesis.
The labeling pattern points first to smooth ER, not to backward movement from the plasma membrane.
Correct. The smooth ER is a major site of lipid synthesis for the endomembrane system.

questions.text: A fluorescent marker is attached to proteins that should remain in the rough ER lumen. In mutant cells, the marker appears outside the cell. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The mutant likely turned the ER into a cell wall.
The mutant likely gained extra lysosomes, which secreted the protein by digestion.
The mutant likely stopped all translation, explaining why the protein was outside the cell.
The mutant likely lost a retention or retrieval mechanism that normally keeps those proteins in the ER.
questions.explain: The mutant likely lost a retention or retrieval mechanism that normally keeps those proteins in the ER.
choice_explanations: The ER does not become a cell wall.
Protein secretion from an ER-resident marker suggests trafficking mis-sorting, not secretion by lysosomal digestion.
If all translation had stopped, the labeled protein would not have been produced and tracked.
Correct. The mutant likely lost a retention or retrieval mechanism that normally keeps those proteins in the ER.

questions.text: In an experiment, chloroplasts are isolated from leaf cells and exposed to light. ATP production occurs only when the thylakoid membranes remain intact. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: ATP production occurs only because chloroplast DNA is attached to the thylakoids.
The thylakoid membranes are needed to maintain conditions required for the light reactions and ATP generation.
The result proves chloroplasts are part of the Golgi apparatus.
The result shows ATP is made in the cell wall of plant cells.
questions.explain: The thylakoid membranes are needed to maintain conditions required for the light reactions and ATP generation.
choice_explanations: Thylakoid membranes are important because they separate compartments and host electron transport, not because chloroplast DNA must be attached to them for ATP production.
Correct. The thylakoid membranes are needed to maintain conditions required for the light reactions and ATP generation.
Chloroplasts are distinct organelles, not part of the Golgi apparatus.
Plant ATP production in photosynthesis is not a cell-wall process.

questions.text: Mitochondria are isolated and the inner membrane is punctured chemically. ATP production drops sharply even though metabolic substrates are still present. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The result means ATP is made in the outer membrane, not the inner membrane.
The puncture stopped transcription in the nucleus, which immediately halted ATP production.
The puncture removed mitochondrial DNA so substrates could no longer diffuse.
An intact inner membrane is needed to maintain the proton gradient used for ATP synthesis.
questions.explain: An intact inner membrane is needed to maintain the proton gradient used for ATP synthesis.
choice_explanations: The sharp drop after inner-membrane disruption argues for the importance of the inner membrane, not the outer membrane, in ATP synthesis.
The isolated organelle experiment points to a local membrane effect, not nuclear transcription.
Loss of a proton gradient, not substrate diffusion alone, is the key issue here.
Correct. An intact inner membrane is needed to maintain the proton gradient used for ATP synthesis.

questions.text: A student stains cells for ribosomes and sees especially strong signal on membranes surrounding the nucleus in secretory cells. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Those membranes are likely lysosomes digesting excess ribosomes.
Those membranes are likely smooth ER, which is covered in ribosomes during secretion.
Those membranes are likely the cell wall, which stores proteins before export.
Those membranes are likely rough ER, which is continuous with the nuclear envelope and rich in bound ribosomes.
questions.explain: Those membranes are likely rough ER, which is continuous with the nuclear envelope and rich in bound ribosomes.
choice_explanations: Lysosomes may digest components, but a ribosome-rich membrane network around the nucleus in secretory cells is rough ER.
Smooth ER lacks bound ribosomes; that is the key difference from rough ER.
Animal secretory cells do not have cell walls, and cell walls do not store proteins for export.
Correct. Those membranes are likely rough ER, which is continuous with the nuclear envelope and rich in bound ribosomes.

questions.text: Researchers add a microtubule inhibitor to ciliated epithelial cells. The cells remain alive, but cilia stop beating. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: Cilia stop because the inhibitor dissolved the cell wall around the cells.
Cilia depend on microtubules for their motility.
The result proves cilia synthesize ATP.
Cilia depend mainly on actin microfilaments arranged like microvilli.
questions.explain: Cilia depend on microtubules for their motility.
choice_explanations: Animal epithelial cells do not have cell walls.
Correct. Cilia depend on microtubules for their motility.
The experiment addresses motility structure, not ATP synthesis by cilia.
Actin supports microvilli and many shape changes, but cilia are built around microtubules.

questions.text: A fluorescent vesicle marker is tracked in living cells. After kinesin inhibition, vesicles accumulate near the center of the cell and fewer reach the plasma membrane. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?
choices: Vesicles normally diffuse randomly and do not depend on the cytoskeleton.
Motor proteins moving along microtubules help deliver many vesicles toward the cell surface.
The result proves exocytosis occurs inside the nucleus.
Kinesin inhibition shows vesicles are made in the plasma membrane.
questions.explain: Motor proteins moving along microtubules help deliver many vesicles toward the cell surface.
choice_explanations: The change in vesicle distribution argues against purely random diffusion as the main mechanism.
Correct. Motor proteins moving along microtubules help deliver many vesicles toward the cell surface.
Exocytosis is release at the plasma membrane, not within the nucleus.
Accumulation near the center points to transport failure, not membrane-based synthesis of vesicles.

questions.text: A layer of epithelial cells is grown on a filter, and a dye is added above the layer. When tight junctions are chemically disrupted, much more dye appears below the layer. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?

questions.text: Plant cells are treated with an enzyme that blocks formation of plasmodesmata in newly dividing tissue. Neighboring cells exchange less fluorescent tracer. Which conclusion is most strongly supported?

questions.text: Researchers compare ribosomes from the cytosol, mitochondria, and bacteria. An antibiotic that blocks many bacterial ribosomes also blocks mitochondrial ribosomes more than cytosolic ribosomes. Which conclusion is best supported?

questions.text: Scientists sequence DNA from chloroplasts and find it is circular rather than linear like most nuclear chromosomes. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: This observation proves chloroplast DNA was copied from the Golgi apparatus.

Chloroplasts are distinct organelles, not simple folds of the plasma membrane.
Circular DNA does not imply inability to divide; if anything, chloroplasts do divide within cells.

questions.text: Prokaryotic cells are engineered to overexpress internal membrane folds, increasing the surface available for electron transport proteins. ATP production rises. Which conclusion is best supported?

Increasing internal membrane area is not the same as evolving a nucleus.

Correct. The result supports the idea that membrane compartmentalization or increased membrane area can enhance certain metabolic processes.
Electron transport still depends on proteins; folding alone is not sufficient.

questions.text: Scientists isolate chloroplasts and remove the thylakoid membranes while leaving the stroma enzymes present. Light-driven ATP formation stops. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The experiment shows chloroplasts are actually mitochondria.
The experiment shows compartmentalized membrane structure is required for this part of photosynthesis.
The experiment shows ATP is made only by DNA in the stroma.
The experiment shows all photosynthetic reactions occur in one undivided fluid space.
questions.explain: The experiment shows compartmentalized membrane structure is required for this part of photosynthesis.
choice_explanations: Loss of thylakoids does not turn chloroplasts into mitochondria.
Correct. The experiment shows compartmentalized membrane structure is required for this part of photosynthesis.
The loss of ATP formation after membrane removal points away from DNA in the stroma as the direct explanation.
The result argues against all reactions occurring effectively in one mixed fluid compartment.

questions.text: When an antibiotic that targets bacterial translation is added to plant cells, production of some chloroplast proteins decreases while most nuclear-encoded cytosolic proteins are less affected. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Chloroplast translation machinery is more similar to bacterial machinery than cytosolic eukaryotic machinery.
The result proves chloroplasts have no ribosomes of their own.
The result means bacterial and plant cells are identical.
The result proves chloroplast proteins are synthesized in the Golgi.
questions.explain: Chloroplast translation machinery is more similar to bacterial machinery than cytosolic eukaryotic machinery.
choice_explanations: Correct. Chloroplast translation machinery is more similar to bacterial machinery than cytosolic eukaryotic machinery.
The result implies chloroplasts do have their own translation machinery, not that they lack it.
Similarity in one feature does not make bacterial and plant cells identical overall.
Differential antibiotic sensitivity points toward chloroplast ribosomes, not Golgi synthesis.

questions.text: Researchers find that proteins performing different tasks are concentrated in different organelles, each with distinct pH and ion conditions. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The cell uses compartmentalization to create specialized environments for different processes.
The cell is inefficient because all reactions would work better in one shared compartment.
The finding proves every organelle contains its own genome.
The distinct environments exist only to keep DNA out of the cytosol.
questions.explain: The cell uses compartmentalization to create specialized environments for different processes.
choice_explanations: Correct. The cell uses compartmentalization to create specialized environments for different processes.
Different pH and ion conditions are often advantageous because they optimize different reactions.
Many organelles do not contain their own genomes.
DNA protection is not the only or main explanation for every specialized compartment.

questions.text: A mitochondrial gene sequence is found to resemble bacterial genes more than most nuclear genes do. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: This pattern is consistent with mitochondria descending from bacterial ancestors.
This pattern shows bacteria are eukaryotes.
This pattern means mitochondria are not inherited during cell division.
This pattern proves the nucleus evolved from mitochondria.
questions.explain: This pattern is consistent with mitochondria descending from bacterial ancestors.
choice_explanations: Correct. This pattern is consistent with mitochondria descending from bacterial ancestors.
Bacteria remain prokaryotes despite evolutionary links to organelles.
Gene similarity does not imply lack of inheritance.
Similarity in mitochondrial genes does not reverse the evolutionary relationship to make the nucleus descend from mitochondria.

questions.text: When hydrogen ions are allowed to leak freely across the inner mitochondrial membrane in isolated mitochondria, ATP output falls even though electron transport components are still present. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The result means the outer membrane normally provides the only compartment that matters.
A membrane-separated proton gradient is necessary for efficient ATP synthesis in mitochondria.
The result shows membranes are unnecessary for energy conversion.
The result proves ATP is synthesized directly by mitochondrial DNA.
questions.explain: A membrane-separated proton gradient is necessary for efficient ATP synthesis in mitochondria.
choice_explanations: The disrupted membrane in the experiment is the inner membrane, highlighting its importance rather than the outer membrane alone.
Correct. A membrane-separated proton gradient is necessary for efficient ATP synthesis in mitochondria.
Loss of ATP when the gradient collapses shows membranes are necessary here, not unnecessary.
The experiment points to a chemiosmotic gradient across a membrane, not direct ATP synthesis by DNA.
